rows.imp_rat: Transferring the finished batch to the wrong downstream destination (item 12) risks cross-contamination and moderate consequences, yet remains a downstream-routing concern relative to the core batching job. Held to the batch limit.
rows.sat_rat: Path verification, destination interlocks and electronic batch records make correct downstream transfer a mature practice, with residual risk mainly during manual reroutes or shared header lines. Automated transfer sequencing routes finished batches to the correct destination without meaningful added delay once paths are validated. Destination logic in the batch engine offloads the decision from the operator, lowering skill dependence for correct dispatch. Sending finished product to the wrong tank risks cross-contamination and loss of a full high-value batch, though such failures are infrequent under interlocked control.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scaled batch quantity falls outside the system's validated operating range
rows.imp_rat: A scaled batch falling outside the validated operating range (instability) is fairly relevant to reliable batching and could cause off-spec product, but the ceiling caps it and validation limits are usually checked before scale-up.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management with configured min/max charge limits catches most out-of-range scaling, but manual scale-ups and undocumented ranges still allow violations. Validated-range checks are near-instant when embedded in recipe management. Recognizing that a scaled quantity exceeds validated limits requires process and validation knowledge, especially outside automated recipe systems. A batch scaled outside the validated range risks scrap and rework, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the scaled batch exceeds validated sterile hold limits before filling
rows.imp_rat: Scaled batch exceeding validated sterile hold limits before filling risks contamination, which carries serious consequences, but relative to the batching CFJ it is a downstream hold-time concern ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Sterile hold-time excursions before filling remain a well-known, poorly buffered failure mode in aseptic operations despite validated limits, so the need is only partly served. The sterile hold clock is tight and any filling-line delay eats into it, making elapsed time a constant pressure. Managing scaled batches within validated sterile hold limits requires trained aseptic-process staff coordinating tightly, a high skill demand. Exceeding sterile hold limits forces rejection of an entire scaled, high-value sterile batch, an expensive and recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising sterile conditions while troubleshooting the batching fault
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile conditions while troubleshooting (contamination-risk) carries high severity because contamination can ruin a batch, but the ceiling caps it and this occurs only during fault handling, limiting relevance to the routine job.
rows.sat_rat: Closed systems, SIP and aseptic SOPs protect sterility, but any physical troubleshooting intervention still carries real contamination risk. Maintaining sterility during troubleshooting adds procedural steps and time. Preserving asepsis while intervening requires trained aseptic-technique discipline. A compromised sterile batch is a total loss plus potential CIP/SIP recovery cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the technician misidentifies the underlying cause of the batching fault
rows.imp_rat: Misidentifying the root cause of a batching fault (defect) has some relevance to restoring correct operation, with severity moderate since a wrong diagnosis prolongs downtime but is usually revisited.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics, historians and alarm logs help, but root-cause identification remains inconsistent and expertise-bound. Fault diagnosis can drag on when the cause is not obvious, extending downtime. Correctly identifying the underlying cause of a batching fault depends heavily on scarce, experienced troubleshooters. Misdiagnosis leads to wasted parts, repeat batches and prolonged downtime cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of altering a critical batch parameter while testing the suspected fault
rows.imp_rat: Altering a critical batch parameter while testing a fault (instability) could spoil the batch, giving meaningful severity, but it is a contained troubleshooting-context risk rather than central to normal batching.
rows.sat_rat: Access controls, permissives and parameter locks reduce accidental changes, but troubleshooting often requires overrides that reopen the risk. Careful testing that avoids disturbing critical parameters slows troubleshooting. Avoiding accidental changes to a critical parameter while probing a fault demands disciplined, knowledgeable technicians. An inadvertent parameter change can ruin the batch in progress, a direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches confirmed at endpoint while defined criteria remain unmet
rows.imp_rat: Confirming batches at endpoint while criteria remain unmet (item 2) directly touches the release decision, which is fairly central, but the severity here is moderated since such false confirmations are usually caught downstream. Held to the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-enforced endpoint criteria reduce premature confirmation, yet partially manual endpoint sign-offs still let some batches through with unmet criteria. Automated endpoint checks confirm criteria without added review time. Configured endpoint logic reduces operator judgement about whether all criteria are met. Prematurely confirmed batches that later fail are expensive, but interlocked endpoints keep this infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of routing the prepared batch to the wrong intermediate vessel
rows.imp_rat: Routing a prepared batch to the wrong intermediate vessel (item 11) can cause mix-ups, giving moderate severity, but it is a handling/routing step of moderate centrality. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Interlocked transfer paths, tag-based valve verification and recipe-driven routing make correct-vessel delivery a well-established, mostly dependable practice with occasional gaps on manual overrides. Automated routing via configured recipes and interlocks executes vessel selection quickly once set up, adding little delay. Modern batch control systems with S88-style phase logic and valve interlocks reduce reliance on operator judgement for correct routing. A misrouted batch can scrap costly product, but interlocks and confirmation prompts make such events relatively uncommon in automated plants.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which critical process conditions deviate from approved limits during manual control
rows.imp_rat: Deviation of critical conditions during manual control (item 17) risks off-spec product, but manual-mode operation is a fallback scenario of moderate relevance. Bounded by the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without automated PID control in the loop, deviations under manual control are frequent and inconsistent, leaving this need poorly served. Manual control keeps the batch running but operator reaction time to correct drifting conditions is slow relative to automated loops. Manual control of critical process conditions leans entirely on scarce, attentive, well-trained operators, making it highly skill-dependent and error-prone. Manual excursions beyond approved limits routinely cause off-spec material and rework, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required pump, valve, or agitator actions omitted or performed out of sequence during the batch step
rows.imp_rat: Omitting or misordering pump, valve, or agitator actions during a batch step directly affects batch correctness, but the ranking caps it below the prior batch's items.
rows.sat_rat: S88 phase logic and equipment interlocks make correctly sequenced actuation a mature, widely deployed practice, with residual risk mostly in manual or degraded modes. Automated phase sequencing executes pump, valve and agitator actions in the right order without slowing the batch. Recipe-driven sequence control removes the need for operators to remember or manually order each actuation, sharply lowering skill dependence. Omitted or out-of-order actions can ruin a batch, but sequence interlocks make such errors uncommon in automated systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of programmed ingredient-addition steps skipped during a batch run
rows.imp_rat: Skipping programmed ingredient-addition steps degrades batch quality with moderate severity, yet the combined ordering places it beneath higher-priority errors, holding it under 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe-managed batch execution with step confirmation and electronic records makes skipped ingredient additions an uncommon, well-controlled failure in automated plants. Programmed addition steps execute automatically in sequence, so no time is lost to operators tracking each step. Automated recipe execution enforces every ingredient-addition step, greatly reducing reliance on operator vigilance to avoid skips. A skipped addition can scrap a batch, but recipe enforcement and step confirmation make skipped steps rare, limiting cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a verification sample changes composition through contamination before analysis
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of a verification sample before analysis (item 1) undermines batch confirmation, but sampling is a peripheral verification step relative to the core batching job; consequences are moderate since a bad sample typically triggers a re-test rather than a released defective batch. Capped at 3.5.
rows.sat_rat: Validated sample containers, chain-of-custody and prompt analysis keep pre-analysis composition change fairly uncommon, though handling and hold-time lapses still occur. Proper sample containers and prompt transfer to the lab add little time. Correct aseptic sample handling requires basic trained technique rather than scarce expertise. Contaminated verification samples trigger costly retesting but such events are individually infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing an incorrect filtration element in the batching system
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incorrect filtration element can compromise sterile assurance (severity), and filtration is relevant to preparation integrity, but this item sits below the higher batch and is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Part-number control, labeling, and kitting make incorrect filter installation relatively uncommon in disciplined operations. Selecting the correct filter is a quick step when part numbers are documented. Matching filter micron rating and type to the recipe requires modest training and reference documentation. Wrong filters waste product batches, but correct elements themselves are low cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that validated recipes, calibration parameters, or audit records are altered during the update
rows.imp_rat: Alteration of validated recipes, calibration, or audit records during an update (item 8) has moderate severity for data integrity, but it is an update-maintenance event peripheral to routine batching. Bounded by the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails, backups, and validated data-integrity controls make unintended alteration of recipes or calibration data rare in mature systems. Backup and verification of records before updates adds modest overhead within the maintenance window. Following data-integrity procedures needs trained personnel but is largely procedural rather than deeply expert. Version control and audit-trail systems are already in place, so incremental cost is low.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the time the measurement device remains in service beyond its approved calibration interval
rows.imp_rat: Running a measurement device past its calibration interval (delay error) is moderately relevant to batching accuracy, but the ceiling caps it; severity is real since out-of-cal data can compromise batch quality yet is often caught in review.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS/calibration-management due-date tracking keeps most devices within interval, though occasional overdue instruments slip through. Automated scheduling minimizes delay in pulling overdue devices from service. Following recall schedules is procedural and requires little judgement. The recall process itself is inexpensive relative to the risk of out-of-cal operation.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of calibration records assigned to the wrong measurement device or reference standard
rows.imp_rat: Calibration records assigned to the wrong device (ambiguity) is a records-integrity error with limited centrality to physical batching, though misattribution could allow a bad instrument to run and affect a batch.
rows.sat_rat: Asset tagging and barcode-linked records make wrong-device record assignment uncommon in electronic systems, though manual entry still errs. Record entry and linking is quick within digital calibration systems. Assigning records correctly is a low-skill data-entry task aided by scanned IDs. Misassignment cost is mostly rework and audit findings rather than material loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of failing to detect wear, leakage, or lubricant degradation during mechanical inspection
rows.imp_rat: Failing to detect wear, leakage, or lubricant degradation during inspection can lead to failures, but as a preventive-maintenance detection gap it is indirect to the batching job and held under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Manual visual/tactile inspection is inconsistent and misses early-stage degradation, making undetected wear a common outcome. Thorough inspection takes time and is often compressed within tight maintenance windows. Detecting subtle wear, incipient leakage, or lubricant degradation depends heavily on inspector experience and judgement. Missed early wear leads to unplanned failures that are far costlier than the inspection itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of existing leaks, corrosion, deterioration, and component wear not detected during the inspection
rows.imp_rat: Missing existing leaks, corrosion, or wear during inspection risks later breakdowns; severity is moderate but relevance to the core batching act is secondary and bounded by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: A meaningful fraction of existing defects routinely goes undetected because manual inspection coverage and consistency are inherently limited. Comprehensive inspection of all surfaces is time-consuming and often abbreviated. Reliably spotting all corrosion, deterioration, and wear across a complex system demands high vigilance and expertise that varies by inspector. Undetected deterioration escalates into larger failures, wasting far more than the inspection cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of installing a sealing component incompatible with the process fluid, operating conditions, or hygienic requirements
rows.imp_rat: Installing an incompatible sealing component can cause failure or contamination, which is fairly severe, but as an installation/materials decision it is upstream of and less central than core batching execution.
rows.sat_rat: BOM/spare-part specifications and compatibility charts prevent most mismatches, yet substitution errors still occur under time pressure. Verifying seal material compatibility adds lookup time but is a discrete pre-task step. Correct seal selection requires materials/process knowledge, though reference charts reduce the expertise burden. Wrong seals cause failures and contamination, but correct parts are inexpensive to specify upfront.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of environmental contaminants entering sterile fluid paths during extended storage
rows.imp_rat: Environmental contaminants entering sterile paths during extended storage has real severity for sterility, but this storage-phase error is less central to the active batching job and is capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Standard sealing practice reduces ingress but extended storage of decommissioned systems still sees contaminant entry when seals degrade unmonitored. Capping and sealing fluid paths for storage is quick with standard closures. Proper sealing and desiccant use is routine technician work needing modest skill. Protective closures and controlled storage add modest cost that is usually accepted.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical instruments with expired or unverified calibration status before run start
rows.imp_rat: Instruments with expired/unverified calibration before run start affect measurement trust (relevance) and could yield wrong readings (severity), but is a readiness check, moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems reliably flag expired instruments where integrated, with gaps for un-tracked devices. Calibration-status checks are fast when tracked in a calibration system. Calibration status flags remove operator judgement about instrument validity. Expired-calibration data can invalidate a batch, mostly prevented by tracking.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of assigned operators with unverified qualification or authorization before run start
rows.imp_rat: Unverified operator qualification before run start is a readiness gate for correct execution (relevance) with potential error consequences (severity), scored moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Training/qualification-linked access control reliably blocks unauthorized operators where integrated. Operator qualification checks at login are near-instant. System authorization gating removes reliance on supervisors verifying credentials. Unqualified operators risk errors and rework, well guarded by access control.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of open batch-impacting deviations lacking approved disposition at readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Open batch-impacting deviations without approved disposition at readiness is a gating concern (relevance) that could let a batch proceed with unresolved risk (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation systems are often separate from MES, so open-deviation blocks are inconsistently enforced at run start. Checking open deviations at readiness requires manual quality-system review that adds time. Judging deviation disposition impact depends on quality expertise. Starting with unresolved deviations risks batch rejection, weakly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed compounding steps lacking execution evidence
rows.imp_rat: Completed steps lacking execution evidence leave record gaps (relevance) and weaken proof of correct execution (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: EBR-enforced evidence capture reliably ensures completed steps carry execution evidence. Automated evidence capture at step completion adds no time. System capture removes operator burden of documenting each step. Missing evidence forces rework or rejection, mostly avoided by auto-capture.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compounding step is recorded as complete before execution finishes
rows.imp_rat: Recording a step complete before execution finishes falsifies the record (relevance) and can advance the batch prematurely (severity), but is a bounded sequencing defect.
rows.sat_rat: EBR completion gating reliably prevents recording a step complete before execution finishes. Completion signing occurs at the actual step end with system timestamps. System-enforced completion sign-off reduces reliance on operator sequencing discipline. Premature completion records cause deviations, mostly prevented by gating.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of execution records attributed to the wrong batch, step, or operator
rows.imp_rat: Execution records attributed to the wrong batch/step/operator corrupt traceability (relevance) and mislead review (severity), kept moderate-low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: MES reliably attributes records to the correct batch, step, and logged-in operator. Automatic attribution tagging is instantaneous. System context binding removes operator responsibility for correct attribution. Misattributed records cause investigations, mostly prevented by auto-tagging.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding deviations and interventions left unrecorded
rows.imp_rat: Unrecorded deviations and interventions leave gaps in the batch record (relevance) and can hide quality-relevant events (severity), but sit below top batch items.
rows.sat_rat: System prompts capture flagged deviations, but operator-observed interventions still go unrecorded. Recording deviations in real time takes operator effort that can lag the event. Recognizing what counts as a recordable deviation depends on operator judgement. Unrecorded deviations cause compliance and quality gaps, partly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required instruments with expired, missing, or unacceptable calibration or qualification status
rows.imp_rat: Instruments with bad calibration/qualification status affect measurement trust (relevance) and could yield wrong enforcement (severity), but is a readiness check, moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration interlocks catch many expired instruments, but not all devices are linked to execution gating. Calibration status lookups are quick where instruments are tracked in a system. Automated calibration flags reduce reliance on operators knowing instrument status. Calibration management systems add cost but are common in regulated sites.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required precondition values missing or outside recipe-defined ranges before execution
rows.imp_rat: Missing/out-of-range precondition values before execution is a readiness gate (relevance) that could start a bad batch (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Configured precondition gates are reliable, but not every precondition is systematized, leaving gaps. Precondition checks at step start avoid downstream delays from bad starting values. Automated precondition gating reduces reliance on operator verification. Precondition rules are configured in the recipe once.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of step confirmations attributed to the wrong batch, vessel, or recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Step confirmations attributed to the wrong batch/vessel/step corrupt the record (relevance) and mislead traceability (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Context binding makes misattributed confirmations rare where scanning and login are enforced. Session-bound context assigns confirmations instantly to the right target. Automated attribution relieves operators of manual tagging. Part of the standard MES session model.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps confirmed outside the required execution sequence
rows.imp_rat: Steps confirmed outside the required sequence violate execution order (relevance) and can affect product quality (severity), but ranked below batch 1, moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Enforced execution order makes out-of-sequence confirmations uncommon in configured EBRs. Sequence enforcement prevents out-of-order confirmation without slowing the flow. Interlocked sequencing removes the need for operators to police step order. Sequence logic is standard recipe configuration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time from a parameter leaving expected conditions until the executor recognizes the deviation
rows.imp_rat: Delay until the executor recognizes a parameter leaving expected conditions prolongs uncontrolled state (relevance) and can worsen the batch (severity), moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm-driven recognition is consistent for instrumented parameters but blind to unsensed conditions. SCADA alarming shortens the interval from deviation to operator awareness. Automated detection lessens dependence on operator attention. Uses installed control and alarm infrastructure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation entries linked to the wrong compounding batch or recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Deviation entries linked to the wrong batch/step mislead investigations (relevance) and can misdirect corrective action (severity), but remain recoverable, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviations are frequently linked to the wrong batch or step because the association is entered manually. Deviation linking is often a manual post-hoc step, adding delay. Correctly associating a deviation to batch and step relies on operator understanding. Deviation management modules add licensing cost not all sites deploy.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of parameter excursions beyond defined acceptable ranges during the compounding run
rows.imp_rat: Frequent parameter excursions beyond acceptable ranges signal enforcement instability (relevance) and can degrade quality (severity), held moderate-low under ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Regulatory control holds parameters in range well, though disturbances and manual steps still cause excursions. Automated control loops correct excursions quickly to keep parameters in range. Closed-loop control reduces reliance on manual operator adjustment. Control loops are part of the existing automation investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between a parameter leaving its acceptable range and the executor recognizing the excursion
rows.imp_rat: Delay in the executor recognizing an out-of-range excursion prolongs uncontrolled conditions (relevance) and can worsen impact (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time alarms make excursion recognition timely for monitored parameters, though unmonitored ones lag. Continuous monitoring alerts the operator quickly when a parameter leaves range. Automated excursion alarms reduce reliance on operator vigilance. Alarming rides on existing control system capability.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of captured entries attributed to the wrong batch, material, or compounding unit
rows.imp_rat: Misattributed captured entries harm the record-keeping half of the job (relevance) and mislead traceability (severity), but are recoverable administrative errors, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Barcode-bound context makes wrong-batch attribution uncommon where scanning is enforced. Barcode-driven context assignment is instantaneous at the point of action. Scanning batch and material IDs removes reliance on operators to remember attribution. Scanning hardware is inexpensive and widely deployed.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of completed material additions omitted from the compounding batch record
rows.imp_rat: Completed material additions omitted from the batch record undercut recording accuracy (relevance) and traceability (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow-enforced additions are seldom omitted, though manual additions outside the flow can be missed. Scan-to-add workflows record additions in real time, leaving few unlogged. Enforced dispensing steps reduce reliance on operators to remember to log each addition. Dispensing integration uses existing weigh and scan equipment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material additions linked to the wrong recipe requirement or step
rows.imp_rat: Additions linked to the wrong recipe requirement/step misrecord execution (relevance) and can misstate compliance (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement linkage is reliable within the workflow but weaker for out-of-flow additions. Workflow linkage ties additions to the right requirement in real time. Guided dispensing reduces reliance on operators mapping material to step. Part of standard recipe and dispensing configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter records attributed to the wrong batch, material, vessel, or recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Parameter records tied to the wrong batch/vessel/step weaken traceability (relevance) and can mislead investigations (severity), yet remain below top-tier items.
rows.sat_rat: Automated context binding makes misattribution rare, though manual entries outside the workflow can still be misfiled. Context is bound automatically when the operator logs into a batch and vessel. Session-bound attribution reduces the operator's memory load for tagging records. Attribution logic is part of the standard MES configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding events with conflicting parameter records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting parameter records create ambiguity in the recorded process (relevance) and can confuse review, but rarely cause direct product harm (severity), placing it low.
rows.sat_rat: Single-source capture reduces conflicts, but parallel paper or auxiliary logs still produce contradictions. A single system-of-record reduces the reconciliation delay from conflicting sources. Centralized records lower the effort of resolving which value is correct. Consolidating records into one MES avoids the cost of maintaining parallel logs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batch record entries lacking attributable performer or approver identity
rows.imp_rat: Entries lacking attributable performer/approver identity weaken accountability (relevance) and impair audit (severity), kept moderate-low under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic signature requirements reliably attach performer and approver identity, making missing attribution rare. Login-based e-signatures capture identity instantly at each entry. Enforced authentication removes any operator skill needed to attribute entries. Unattributed entries fail audits, but e-signature enforcement prevents most.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compounding parameter readings unavailable during recipe execution
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable parameter readings during execution impair enforcement visibility (relevance) and can leave excursions undetected (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Integrated instrumentation supplies most required readings, but sensor faults and manual measurements still leave occasional gaps. Missing readings during execution force pauses to retrieve or re-measure, adding delay. Filling gaps requires the operator to know which readings are needed and how to obtain them. Unavailable readings can stall a batch, a throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter readings attributed to the wrong batch, vessel, or recipe step during the shift
rows.imp_rat: Parameter readings misattributed to the wrong batch/vessel/step during the shift weaken the record (relevance) and mislead review (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Where equipment is integrated, readings map reliably to batch and step, though manual entries and shared vessels still cause occasional misattribution. System-tagged readings are attributed automatically with no added time. Automatic tagging by vessel and step reduces the operator's burden to attribute readings correctly. Misattributed readings can cause investigations, but automation limits frequency.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required compounding steps and parameters omitted from the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required steps/parameters from the batch record leaves documentation incomplete (relevance) and weakens reconstruction (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: MES that gates progression on completed entries makes omitted required steps fairly uncommon, so this is reasonably served. Enforced electronic sequencing prompts each step in turn, adding little time. Step-by-step EBR guidance reduces reliance on the operator remembering every required entry. Missing steps cause deviations, but enforced workflows keep these relatively rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviations, alarms, and manual interventions omitted from batch documentation
rows.imp_rat: Omitting deviations, alarms, and interventions from batch documentation weakens the record (relevance) and impairs later review (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Manual interventions and alarms are frequently under-documented despite EBR prompts, leaving recurring gaps. Reconstructing omitted alarms and interventions after the fact is time-consuming during record review. Complete documentation depends on the operator's diligence to log every manual intervention amid the work. Omissions trigger deviation investigations and rework, a documentation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between detection of a recipe or parameter deviation and required escalation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between deviation detection and escalation slows response (relevance) and can let excursions persist (severity), but detection itself already occurred, moderating both.
rows.sat_rat: Alert-to-escalation handoffs are inconsistently automated, so escalation timeliness varies widely by shift and site. Automated alerting can trigger immediately, but escalation still often depends on manual routing and human acknowledgement. Judging severity and the correct escalation path relies on experienced staff. Delayed escalation extends exposure and increases containment cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required record attributes and traceability links the recommended approach cannot preserve
rows.imp_rat: Inability to preserve record attributes/traceability links weakens documentation completeness (relevance) and hampers reconstruction (severity), but is a capability gap, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporary systems preserve many record attributes and links, yet completeness against specific traceability requirements is inconsistently verified. Checking that every record attribute and traceability link is preserved is detailed work often abbreviated. Knowing which record attributes and links are required needs data-integrity expertise not uniformly held. Unpreserved attributes create costly remediation and re-documentation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that conflicting compounding records obscure the authoritative recipe value
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting records obscuring the authoritative recipe value risks executing the wrong value (relevance) with quality consequences (severity), kept moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple record sources still obscure the authoritative recipe value more often than acceptable. Untangling conflicting records to find the authoritative value adds investigation time. Identifying the true authoritative value requires skilled record analysis. Acting on a wrong value from obscured records carries batch-loss cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batches with conflicting evidence about the recipe and parameter requirements in effect
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting evidence about the in-effect recipe/parameter requirements creates enforcement ambiguity (relevance) that could cause wrong execution (severity), held moderate-low.
rows.sat_rat: Conflicting evidence about requirements in effect is common and not systematically resolved. Resolving conflicting evidence about requirements in effect is a slow investigative task. Determining the authoritative requirement demands experienced interpretation. Conflicting evidence prolongs disposition and risks costly wrong decisions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the allowable batch hold limit while completing the step manually
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding the allowable hold limit while completing a step manually (item 18) can compromise a batch, but it is a manual-fallback timing concern of moderate relevance and modest severity. Within cap.
rows.sat_rat: Timers and alarms exist to flag approaching hold limits, but manual completion means breaches still happen with real frequency. The core error is elapsed time exceeding a hold limit while a step is done manually, and manual execution is inherently slow and variable. Beating the hold clock manually requires practiced operators who work efficiently under pressure, so skill matters. Exceeding hold limits forces disposition or scrap of the batch, a recurring cost when steps run long.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between recognizing a process deviation and completing the required setpoint correction
rows.imp_rat: Time between recognizing a process deviation and completing setpoint correction affects responsiveness, but modest delays rarely cause irreversible harm to the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Standard alarm-to-action workflows are well established, though inconsistent operator response and procedure gaps leave residual variability. Recognizing a deviation and executing the setpoint correction is fast on modern HMIs, though root-cause confirmation before acting adds delay. The correction magnitude depends on operator judgement, so response time varies with expertise. Prompt correction bounds off-spec production, a meaningful but not dominant saving.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of process-parameter oscillation caused by competing setpoint changes during an active batch
rows.imp_rat: Parameter oscillation from competing setpoint changes degrades batch stability, but the effect is typically transient and correctable, keeping both proxies modest.
rows.sat_rat: PID tuning, cascade control and interlock logic in standard DCS/PLC systems dampen most oscillation, though poorly tuned loops and manual overrides still cause recurring hunting. Modern batch controllers apply setpoint changes within seconds, so oscillation is not primarily a delay problem. Avoiding competing setpoint changes still leans on experienced operators who understand loop interactions and hold discipline during a batch. Oscillation wastes reagents and energy through overcorrection, but the recurring cost is modest relative to a lost batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of loose, degraded, or corroded batching-system connections remaining undetected after inspection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected loose or corroded connections after inspection can cause leaks, a moderately severe outcome, but this inspection-quality error is peripheral to the batching CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Without instrumented connection monitoring, degraded or loose connections are commonly missed during walkdown inspections. Checking every connection is tedious and frequently rushed during turnarounds. Identifying loose or corroded connections relies on systematic technician attention that is easy to lapse. Undetected bad connections cause later leaks and failures, generating repeat callouts and downtime.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation from the specified tolerance after calibration
rows.imp_rat: Deviation from specified tolerance after calibration affects measurement accuracy, but it is a peripheral maintenance-quality issue with limited direct stakes for a single batch.
rows.sat_rat: Traceable reference standards and documented calibration procedures reliably bring devices well within tolerance in mature programs. Calibration procedures are well-defined and executed within scheduled service time. Standardized calibration methods and instruments reduce reliance on individual expertise. Calibration consumables and instrument time are modest and predictable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that accessing tanks, piping, pumps, valves, mixers, sensors, or fittings for inspection causes damage or misalignment
rows.imp_rat: Damage or misalignment from accessing components for inspection (rework) is a maintenance-handling error peripheral to producing a batch, with consequences usually limited to repair rework.
rows.sat_rat: Procedures and access aids reduce incidents, but accidental damage or disturbed alignment during access still occurs with some regularity. Careful access to avoid damage slows the inspection but is factored into the plan. Accessing sensitive components without causing damage or misalignment relies on experienced, careful handling. Access-induced damage creates avoidable repair costs and additional downtime.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of internal zones left untreated because they cannot be accessed during decontamination
rows.imp_rat: Untreated internal zones during decontamination can seed contamination, but this is a cleaning/access issue tangential to the batching CFJ and constrained by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard CIP/SIP routines are designed for production, not disposal, so internal zones routinely go untreated with no reliable verification at end of life. Decontaminating dead legs and inaccessible internal zones adds significant time as crews improvise access or extend flush cycles. Identifying and reaching untreated internal zones on aging, undocumented systems demands scarce process and mechanical expertise. Disassembly, extra cleaning agents, and rework to reach hidden zones raise disposal cost materially.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production disruption caused by system removal and handover
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption from system removal and handover (delay) is a transitional event not part of routine batching; its severity is bounded because it is a planned, scheduled downtime rather than an unexpected failure.
rows.sat_rat: Coordinated removal plans usually keep disruption bounded, though unforeseen tie-ins extend interruptions. System removal and handover directly consumes production time that planned-shutdown scheduling minimizes but cannot eliminate. Rigging and safe removal of large batching skids needs specialized crews. Downtime and specialist removal labor represent significant but planned costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of sterile fill-finish batching interruption during the transition
rows.imp_rat: Batching interruption during transition (delay) is a one-time changeover concern peripheral to the ongoing CFJ, with severity limited to planned lost-production time.
rows.sat_rat: Transition planning generally keeps interruptions predictable, with occasional overruns. Batching interruption during transition is scheduled into changeover windows to limit duration. Sequencing the transition to protect ongoing batches needs experienced production planners. Interrupted batching means idle lines, a real but anticipated cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation between the executed batch sequence and the approved sequence during the validation batch
rows.imp_rat: Deviation between executed and approved sequence during validation affects qualification, but it is a bounded validation-batch event with limited recurring consequence to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Automated batch execution engines enforce approved sequences closely, so deviations during the validation batch are comparatively well controlled. Comparing executed to approved sequence during validation is a defined but time-consuming review. Detecting sequence deviations relies on validation staff familiar with the approved procedure. Sequence deviations invalidate the batch and force costly re-execution.

rows.stmt: Minimize the time between detecting a consistency deviation and applying the corresponding parameter adjustment
rows.imp_rat: Delay between detecting a consistency deviation and applying adjustment slightly worsens product quality, but short lags are typically absorbed without lasting damage.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop control responds consistently where deployed, but reliance on lab or grab-sample results introduces variable delays and misses. Inline sensors and automated control shrink detect-to-adjust latency to seconds when instrumented, though manual sampling loops still add minutes. Automated correction reduces reliance on an operator to interpret and act, but manual regimes still demand attentive judgement. Faster correction limits off-spec volume, saving material without major added expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of consistency oscillations caused by successive parameter adjustments during the batch
rows.imp_rat: Consistency oscillations from successive adjustments reduce product uniformity, but such oscillations are usually damped and recoverable, so relevance and severity remain low-moderate.
rows.sat_rat: Deadband, rate limits and inline viscosity/consistency sensing curb chasing, yet lagging feedback still lets successive adjustments overshoot fairly often. Consistency responses take real settling time and successive adjustments before the loop stabilizes, but the core issue is variability not speed. Knowing to wait for a process to settle before the next adjustment is an experience-dependent skill that inexperienced operators frequently lack. Repeated adjustments consume added additive and mixing energy, a moderate recurring waste.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator pauses the wrong batch cycle during an urgent intervention
rows.imp_rat: Pausing the wrong batch during an urgent intervention has moderate severity and some relevance to safe operation, but as an operator-action error it is capped below the higher batch.
rows.sat_rat: Unit-specific HMI displays and clear batch tagging reduce mis-selection, but crowded multi-unit screens still allow wrong-batch pauses during urgent intervention. Selecting the right cycle to pause under urgency can cost seconds of navigation, a minor delay in a crisis. Correctly identifying the target batch among several depends on operator familiarity with the interface and unit naming, a real skill demand under pressure. Pausing the wrong batch can disrupt or spoil an unrelated batch, an infrequent but costly mistake.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of uncertainty about whether the active batch cycle has fully paused
rows.imp_rat: Uncertainty about whether the batch cycle has fully paused is relevant to safe intervention and carries moderate severity, but it is a peripheral confirmation error ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Modern HMIs give explicit hold/paused state indication, making pause confirmation generally clear, though ambiguous or lagging indicators still occur in older or partial systems. State feedback appears on the HMI quickly after a pause, so operators are not left waiting long to know the status. Reading pause/hold status from the HMI requires minimal skill given standard status indicators. Uncertainty about pause state could prompt costly wrong actions, but clear status displays keep such errors limited.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning the wrong initiating event when responses cascade through the batching system
rows.imp_rat: Assigning the wrong initiating event in a cascade (ambiguity) is a diagnostic-analysis error peripheral to the batching CFJ itself, with consequences mostly extended troubleshooting effort.
rows.sat_rat: Sequence-of-events recording and time-stamped logs help where present, but many systems lack the resolution to reliably isolate the first cause. Untangling a cascade to find the initiating event is time-consuming during a trip. Assigning the true first-cause among cascading responses is a demanding diagnostic skill few operators possess. Chasing the wrong initiating event wastes labor and can trigger unnecessary corrective spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between indicated batch status and the actual process state
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between indicated and actual batch status can mislead operators, but such gaps are often reconciled through checks, giving moderate-low relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: State machines and batch execution software (e.g. S88) track status well, yet sensor faults and unmodeled states still create discrepancies between indication and reality. Status updates on the HMI are near real-time, so lag is minor. Interpreting whether indicated status truly matches the physical state still requires process understanding. Acting on a false status can waste a batch, but the monitoring itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that required ingredients are unavailable in sufficient quantities for the enlarged batch
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient unavailability for an enlarged batch (delay) relates to scale-up logistics rather than core batching function, and the consequence is typically a schedule slip rather than a ruined product.
rows.sat_rat: ERP/MRP and inventory systems reliably flag shortages before batching, though manual dispensing errors and unlogged stock still occasionally cause surprises. Checking ingredient availability before scaling is a quick step in most planning workflows. Inventory checks are routine and require little specialized skill. Holding sufficient inventory for enlarged batches ties up working capital.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of the batch exceeding allowable hold limits before the batching fault is resolved
rows.imp_rat: The batch exceeding hold limits before a fault is resolved (item 20) can waste material, but it is a fault-recovery timing concern of moderate relevance and modest severity. Held to the 3.5 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Fault diagnosis and repair times vary wildly with no standard fast-recovery practice, so hold-limit breaches during faults are a frequent, poorly served pain. The clock keeps running while a batching fault is diagnosed and fixed, and fault resolution time is unpredictable and often long. Fast fault resolution requires scarce troubleshooting expertise, so the outcome hinges heavily on who is available. Unresolved faults that push a batch past hold limits routinely destroy batch value, a significant recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batching tasks performed out of the required sequence during multi-operator production
rows.imp_rat: Out-of-sequence batching tasks in multi-operator production can cause errors, but they are usually recoverable procedural slips with moderate relevance to the core batching job.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic work instructions and sequence enforcement help, but human handoffs in multi-operator production still produce out-of-sequence execution with notable frequency. Out-of-sequence tasks across operators cause rework and waiting, adding avoidable time in multi-operator runs. Keeping the required task order across multiple operators depends heavily on procedural discipline and coordination skill. Sequence errors can spoil batches or require reprocessing, a recurring cost in busy multi-operator production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of disagreement among process readings used to verify the batch endpoint
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement among readings used to verify endpoint complicates confirmation, but it is a bounded verification-step issue with limited consequence to the overall job.
rows.sat_rat: Multi-parameter endpoint checks and redundant sensors reduce disagreement, but calibration drift and single-point measurements still leave conflicting readings unresolved. Reconciling disagreeing readings at endpoint takes extra verification time. Judging which reading to trust when instruments disagree is a distinctly skill-dependent call. Redundant instrumentation to reduce disagreement adds capital cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that endpoint criteria cannot be matched unambiguously to the available process readings
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous mapping of endpoint criteria to process readings (item 3) can delay confirmation, but this is largely a definitional/configuration concern with moderate relevance and modest, recoverable consequences. Within the 3.5 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Endpoint criteria often reference qualities not directly measured by installed sensors, leaving recurring interpretive ambiguity. Mapping endpoint criteria to available readings is quick when instrumentation is well-defined. Interpreting whether existing process readings unambiguously satisfy endpoint criteria demands engineering judgement. Ambiguity causes disputed dispositions and delays rather than large direct material cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of unintended residual fluid trapped at piping connections after transfer or cleaning
rows.imp_rat: Residual fluid trapped at piping connections is relevant to cleaning and cross-contamination with moderate severity, but this hygienic-design detail ranks below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Hygienic connection design mitigates trapping, but field connections still trap fluid inconsistently, so the need is only partially served. Draining and verifying no trapped fluid at connections adds handling time. Recognizing and clearing dead-leg trapping requires hygienic-design and operator knowledge. Trapped residual fluid causes contamination and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that obsolete or unapproved recipe versions are designated as current after import
rows.imp_rat: Designating obsolete recipe versions as current after import is relevant to formulation correctness with moderate severity, but as an import-administration error it ranks below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Import routines often don't enforce approval status automatically, so unapproved versions can be flagged current, leaving a recurring gap. Confirming the approval status of an imported version after import adds a verification step. It takes quality/change-control judgement to distinguish approved from unapproved versions during import. Designating an unapproved version as current risks scrapped batches and compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that authorized users cannot perform assigned control-system tasks because required permissions are missing
rows.imp_rat: Missing permissions blocking authorized users (defect) is largely an access-administration issue peripheral to the batching CFJ, with modest consequences as it usually causes short workflow delays rather than batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access provisioning is standard practice yet role definitions are frequently incomplete at preparation time, so authorized users still routinely hit missing-permission walls. Missing permissions surface as work stoppages that must be escalated and remediated, adding unpredictable delay during commissioning. Correctly mapping assigned control-system tasks to the exact permission set requires administrators who understand both the DCS role model and the operational duties, a combination that is often thin. Blocked authorized users idle expensive commissioning resources, but the incremental money lost per incident is usually modest.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of operating limits outside validated ranges for the handled liquid and sterile preparation process
rows.imp_rat: Operating limits outside validated ranges is relevant to process compliance with moderate severity, but overlaps other items and ranks below the prior batch, so it is capped.
rows.sat_rat: Alignment of configured limits to validated ranges relies on manual cross-referencing to validation documents, so mismatches persist and the need is poorly-to-acceptably served. Verifying that limits match validated ranges for a specific sterile process is a detailed, time-consuming review. This requires specialized sterile-process and validation expertise that is scarce. Limits outside validated ranges risk failed sterile batches with high scrap and reprocessing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the combined capacity, flow, and timing limits make the required batch cycle infeasible
rows.imp_rat: Infeasible batch cycles from combined capacity/flow/timing limits are a design-phase constraint, largely resolved before production, keeping relevance to routine execution low.
rows.sat_rat: Established sizing calculations and simulation tools make most cycle-time bottlenecks visible before startup, though hidden interaction effects still occasionally render a required cycle infeasible. Capacity and flow feasibility checks during preparation are typically done up front through process calculations, so they rarely add delay once the design basis is set. Verifying that combined capacity, flow, and timing limits support the batch cycle demands experienced process engineers whose judgement is scarce and not fully proceduralized. A missed feasibility limit surfaces late and forces costly rework or equipment changes, but this is caught often enough that catastrophic overspend is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required materials that are unreleased, expired, quarantined, or unavailable before run start
rows.imp_rat: Unreleased/expired/quarantined materials before run start is a readiness gating concern (relevance); if bypassed it risks bad material (severity), but the check is preventive, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Inventory release-status gating reliably blocks unreleased or expired materials before run start. Material-readiness checks are quick where inventory is integrated. System status flags reduce operator responsibility for material readiness. Unavailable or quarantined materials cause delays and loss, mostly guarded.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which room or utility conditions deviate from approved limits at readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Room/utility conditions deviating at readiness confirmation is an environmental precondition (relevance) that could affect quality (severity), but is a preventive check, low.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring reliably reports room conditions, though gating on them at run start is uneven. Room/utility condition checks at readiness are near-real-time via SCADA. Automated environmental monitoring reduces operator judgement on readiness conditions. Out-of-limit conditions at start risk the batch, partly guarded.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of active-run stoppages caused by unavailable recipe execution
rows.imp_rat: Active-run stoppages from unavailable recipe execution disrupt runs (relevance) and could jeopardize in-progress batches (severity), but is largely availability-driven, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: System redundancy reduces execution outages, but SCADA/MES downtime still halts runs occasionally. Recipe-execution unavailability stops active runs, with recovery time varying. Restoring execution may require IT/engineering support beyond the operator. Active-run stoppages waste in-process material, a significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which environmental conditions or supporting utilities deviate from recipe-defined limits before execution
rows.imp_rat: Environmental/utility deviations before execution is a precondition concern (relevance) that could affect quality (severity), but is preventive, scored low.
rows.sat_rat: EMS integration flags many out-of-limit conditions, but not all utilities are interlocked to execution start. Environmental monitoring provides near-real-time status before execution. Automated utility and environmental checks reduce operator judgement. Environmental monitoring infrastructure is a significant but common installed cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a recipe departure and its documentation in the compounding batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay documenting a recipe departure risks contemporaneous accuracy (relevance) and could obscure the deviation (severity), but the departure is still captured, low.
rows.sat_rat: Delayed departure documentation increases the chance detail is lost or inaccurate. The gap between a departure and its documentation is often long because logging is manual and deferred. Prompt documentation depends on operator diligence amid active processing. No dedicated real-time departure capture exists in most systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records missing required event, condition, action, or disposition details
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records missing event/action/disposition detail reduce record completeness (relevance) and hamper follow-up (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Required-field templates help, but deviation records still frequently miss condition or disposition detail. Templated deviation forms prompt fields but still require after-the-fact completion. Deciding what event, action and disposition details matter depends on operator experience. Form templates are a modest configuration cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records assigned an incorrect occurrence timestamp
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect occurrence timestamps on deviation records weaken chronology (relevance) and can mislead investigations (severity), but are a bounded record defect.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation occurrence times are commonly inaccurate because they are reconstructed rather than captured automatically. Occurrence timestamps are often entered from memory well after the event. Judging the true occurrence time depends on operator recall. Timestamp capture for deviations lacks dedicated tooling in many systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps unintentionally executed more than once during a compounding batch
rows.imp_rat: Steps unintentionally executed twice cause rework (relevance) and could over-process or double-dose (severity), but is a contained execution error, low.
rows.sat_rat: Completed-step locking makes unintended re-execution uncommon where interlocks exist. State tracking prevents accidental re-execution without added delay. System state flags reduce reliance on operators to track completed steps. Step-state logic is included in recipe configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of alarm responses recorded with missing or incorrect action, sequence, or disposition details
rows.imp_rat: Alarm responses with missing/incorrect detail leave documentation gaps (relevance) that can obscure response adequacy (severity), but are peripheral, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm response records commonly miss action, sequence, or disposition detail because capture is manual and post-event. Alarm responses are often logged after handling, delaying accurate detail capture. Recording correct action and sequence depends on operator recall under pressure. Alarm-response documentation tooling is limited in typical setups.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between executing a compounding action and documenting it in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay between executing an action and documenting it risks record accuracy/contemporaneity (relevance) and can lose detail (severity), but is a timing gap, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: EBR encourages at-the-moment recording, though manual steps and busy periods still stretch the gap sometimes. Contemporaneous electronic entry keeps the gap between action and documentation short. Guided real-time prompts reduce reliance on operators remembering to record later. Delayed documentation causes reconstruction cost, which EBR contemporaneity reduces.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch events documented with an incorrect timestamp or sequence
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect timestamps/sequence on batch events distort chronology (relevance) and can mislead reconstruction (severity), but are a bounded record defect.
rows.sat_rat: Synchronized system time in EBR makes incorrect timestamps or sequence uncommon, so this is well served. System clocks stamp events instantly, eliminating timing delay. Automatic timestamping removes reliance on operators recording sequence manually. Timestamp errors trigger investigations, but automation keeps them rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of disagreement about recipe state, executed parameters, and batch impact during a disruption
rows.imp_rat: Disagreement about recipe state during a disruption can slow correct response (relevance) and confuse impact assessment (severity), but is a transient coordination matter.
rows.sat_rat: With data split across MES, paper, and memory, disagreement about executed state during disruptions is common and poorly resolved. Reconciling differing accounts of recipe state during a disruption consumes time in meetings and record hunts. Establishing a shared truth requires interpreting fragmented data across roles, a skill-intensive reconciliation. Disagreement delays decisions and risks wrong disposition, driving cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved excursions omitted from shift handoff information
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved excursions missing from shift handoff is a communication gap (relevance) that could delay resolution (severity), but is peripheral to core execution.
rows.sat_rat: Informal or minimally structured handoffs routinely lose unresolved items, making this poorly served. Handoff briefings are rushed at shift change, leaving little time to convey every unresolved excursion. Complete handoff depends on the outgoing overseer's recall and thoroughness, a fragile human factor. A dropped excursion can escalate on the next shift, causing costly downstream failure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of excursions not linked to the applicable recipe step and affected product units
rows.imp_rat: Excursions not linked to recipe step/affected units weaken impact tracing (relevance) and could understate affected product (severity), kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Step-level and unit-level traceability of excursions is inconsistent, leaving many excursions poorly linked. Linking excursions to specific steps and affected units requires time-consuming manual correlation. Mapping an excursion to the applicable recipe step and impacted units needs process expertise. Poor linkage over- or under-scopes impacted product, driving unnecessary cost or risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among event timestamps, batch identifiers, material lots, recipe revisions, and parameter records
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among timestamps/IDs/lots/revisions weakens record coherence (relevance) and can mislead reconstruction (severity), but is a bounded data-quality issue.
rows.sat_rat: Multiple unsynchronized systems produce timestamp and identifier mismatches that persist as a common data-integrity issue. Reconciling timestamps and identifiers across systems is a slow manual chore. Detecting and resolving inconsistencies requires analysts familiar with each system's quirks. Inconsistent data prolongs investigations and inflates labor cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that related batches, material lots, recipe revisions, equipment, or processing stages are omitted from investigation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting related batches/lots/revisions from investigation scope is an investigation-thoroughness concern (relevance) that could miss impact (severity), but peripheral to execution.
rows.sat_rat: Genealogy and cross-linking are often fragmented, so relevant related batches, lots and equipment are frequently missed from scope. Assembling all related entities into scope is a slow, largely manual effort. Determining complete investigation scope depends heavily on investigator expertise and system knowledge. Omitted scope causes repeat investigations and expanded impact cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among recipe controls derived from the same applicable requirement
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency among recipe controls from the same requirement introduces enforcement ambiguity (relevance) that could cause misexecution (severity), kept low.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely automated linkage from a requirement to all its derived controls, so inconsistencies commonly go undetected. Checking consistency across derived controls is a manual, periodic effort. Ensuring controls from the same requirement agree needs deep requirements knowledge. Inconsistent controls cause conflicting decisions and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between occurrence of a compounding deviation and its visibility in monitored indicators
rows.imp_rat: Delay between a deviation and its visibility in indicators slows detection (relevance) and can prolong an excursion (severity), but is a monitoring latency, kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Real-time monitoring helps for parameterized signals but many deviations only surface in periodic indicators, so visibility lag remains. Latency between a deviation occurring and its appearance in indicators can be significant due to batch-cycle reporting. Interpreting when an indicator reflects a specific deviation requires analytical skill. Detection lag extends exposure and increases the cost of any resulting containment.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of incompatibilities with compounding equipment and control interfaces remaining unresolved
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved equipment/control-interface incompatibilities are an integration reliability concern (relevance) that can disrupt runs (severity), but are peripheral to the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Interface incompatibilities are partially surfaced through vendor compatibility lists but many remain unresolved until integration is attempted. Resolving equipment and interface incompatibilities requires integration testing that timelines rarely fully allow. Diagnosing control-interface incompatibilities demands specialized integration engineering skill. Unresolved incompatibilities drive expensive custom middleware and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding exceptions with unverified safe recovery and record continuity
rows.imp_rat: Unverified safe recovery/record continuity for exceptions is an edge-case reliability concern (relevance) with potential but limited consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Recovery and record continuity for exceptions is inconsistently verified, so many exception cases remain unproven at recommendation time. Verifying safe recovery and record continuity for exceptions is slow and usually cut short. Confirming safe recovery paths requires operational and control expertise that is scarce. Unverified recovery leads to costly batch loss and record gaps in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required validation evidence that the evaluated approach cannot generate
rows.imp_rat: Validation-evidence generation capability is peripheral to executing recipes (relevance) and its lapse is mainly a documentation gap (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Modern systems generate substantial validation evidence, but gaps in what any given approach can produce are only partially uncovered during assessment. Determining what validation evidence a candidate can generate takes probing that the timeline squeezes. Assessing validation-evidence capability requires CSV expertise that the influencer often must source externally. Missing evidence generation forces expensive manual documentation later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of process parameters with inconsistent units, ranges, or precision between recipes and execution controls
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent units/ranges/precision between recipes and controls introduce ambiguity (relevance) that could cause misexecution (severity), but is a bounded configuration issue.
rows.sat_rat: Unit, range, and precision inconsistencies are common and only partially caught by manual review, so mismatches frequently persist. Reconciling units, ranges, and precision across recipes and controls is tedious and often deferred. Detecting unit and precision mismatches requires meticulous domain and engineering attention that is easy to miss. Undetected mismatches cause costly dosing errors and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of critical recipe behaviors lacking documented test evidence
rows.imp_rat: Critical recipe behaviors lacking test evidence is a validation-completeness concern (relevance) that could leave failure modes unverified (severity), but peripheral to executing runs.
rows.sat_rat: Test protocols and traceability matrices help ensure coverage, but critical behaviors lacking documented evidence still slip through. Documenting test evidence for every critical behavior is laborious and frequently trails execution. Knowing which behaviors are critical and what constitutes adequate evidence requires trained validation judgement. Gaps in evidence are cheap to overlook but costly to remediate during audit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations with undetermined impact on system acceptance
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations with undetermined acceptance impact is an evaluation-completeness concern (relevance) with contained consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation management systems track items but impact determinations frequently remain open or ambiguous at the decision point. Determining deviation impact on acceptance is slow because it waits on investigations and cross-functional input. Assessing undetermined impact requires experienced judgement blending technical and quality perspectives. Unresolved deviations carry rework and delay costs that are hard to bound.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required compliance evidence unavailable or incomplete when acceptance conditions are formulated
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete compliance evidence when acceptance conditions are formulated is an evidence-gathering concern peripheral to execution (relevance) with contained consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Evidence is frequently incomplete when needed because it is scattered and not assembled proactively. Gathering complete compliance evidence at recommendation time is often a slow scramble across systems. Knowing what evidence is required demands regulatory experience that not all reviewers have. Incomplete evidence forces expensive re-reviews and delays batch disposition.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the criteria for determining whether each acceptance condition is satisfied
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in acceptance-condition satisfaction criteria is an evaluation-clarity concern (relevance) that could cause misjudgment (severity), but is peripheral to execution.
rows.sat_rat: Criteria are often qualitatively worded, so satisfaction determinations vary by interpreter. Ambiguous acceptance criteria prolong deliberation as reviewers debate interpretation. Resolving ambiguity leans on senior judgement because criteria are rarely fully operationalized. Ambiguity drives rework and inconsistent decisions that carry downstream cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recommended acceptance conditions lacking traceability to a compliance expectation or identified risk
rows.imp_rat: Acceptance conditions lacking traceability to a compliance expectation is an evidence-linkage gap (relevance) with contained consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Traceability is inconsistently documented, so many conditions lack a clear link to an expectation or risk. Building traceability from each condition back to a requirement is a manual, time-consuming documentation step. Establishing sound traceability requires reviewers who understand both the risk and the regulatory basis. Untraceable conditions provoke audit findings and costly justification exercises.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved deviations or exceptions omitted from the acceptance recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved deviations omitted from the acceptance recommendation is an evaluation-completeness gap (relevance) that could pass unaddressed risk (severity), but peripheral, low.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation-management systems help but linkage into the final recommendation is imperfect, so omissions occur. Tracking every open deviation into the recommendation is a slow manual reconciliation. Ensuring no exception is missed relies on diligent reviewers rather than automated closure gates. Omitted deviations surface later as costly findings and rework.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, process engineers, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking critical sterile fill-finish requirements during proposal comparison
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking sterile fill-finish requirements during proposal comparison (defect) is a procurement-phase concern that is peripheral to executing the batching job, with consequences deferred and correctable before deployment.
rows.sat_rat: Requirement traceability matrices and comparison checklists help, but manual side-by-side review still routinely lets critical items go unnoticed. Thorough proposal comparison against a full requirement set is time-consuming when multiple bids are involved. Spotting an overlooked critical requirement across dense proposals depends on an experienced reviewer's attention and domain knowledge. Comparison effort is largely labor cost; missed requirements later become costly change orders.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe components assigned to incorrect batching-system fields during import
rows.imp_rat: Recipe components mapped to incorrect fields during import is a data-import quality error; moderate severity but peripheral relevance to the physical batching job.
rows.sat_rat: Mapping is largely manual with weak automated validation, so wrong-field assignments slip through and are caught inconsistently, making this a chronic gap. Manual field-by-field mapping during import is slow and verification adds more time. Correct mapping requires someone who understands both the source recipe format and the batching system's field schema. Misassigned components can spoil a batch, wasting ingredients and batch time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of imported recipe and product records with required formulation or product fields missing
rows.imp_rat: Missing required formulation fields in imported records is a data-quality error with limited direct relevance to the physical batching job and modest severity, ranked well below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Import validations and required-field rules catch many gaps, but incomplete source data still passes through, making this acceptably served. Scanning imported records for missing formulation fields adds a review pass. Identifying which formulation/product fields matter requires domain familiarity but is fairly standardized. Missing fields caught early are cheap; propagated to production they cost batches.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of ingredient-source routes with one or more required assets omitted
rows.imp_rat: Ingredient-source routes missing required assets create configuration gaps, but this is a setup/mapping error of limited severity and modest centrality to executing the batch.
rows.sat_rat: Today route definitions are often built from tribal knowledge and spreadsheets, so omissions recur and are only caught downstream, leaving this need poorly served. Assembling ingredient-source routes with all required assets is manually time-consuming and re-checking omissions adds further delay. Catching an omitted asset on a route depends heavily on an experienced engineer's familiarity with the plant layout and material flow. Missed assets discovered late force rework and can idle the line, incurring real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient-source associations that conflict across piping, equipment, and control records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting ingredient-source associations across records (item 15) is a data-consistency concern with low direct relevance to executing a batch and modest traceability consequences. Held low.
rows.sat_rat: With disconnected P&IDs, asset registers and control configs, conflicting source associations persist and are a chronic, poorly served gap. Reconciling conflicting source records across systems is a slow, mostly manual cross-check. Resolving conflicts between piping, equipment and control records demands expert knowledge of all three domains. Record reconciliation is labor cost more than material, but conflicts cause costly errors when unresolved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient-source mappings left obsolete after design changes
rows.imp_rat: Obsolete ingredient-source mappings after design changes create rework, but this is a documentation-maintenance error peripheral to producing a correct batch.
rows.sat_rat: Change management frequently fails to purge obsolete source mappings, making this one of the least reliably served needs on the list. Obsolete mappings surface only during later troubleshooting, adding investigation time. Detecting stale mappings after design changes relies on diligent change-management discipline that is easily neglected. Maintaining current mappings is low direct cost but stale data drives expensive errors.
