rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operating limits based on ambiguous or mismatched units and reference bases
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous units or reference bases in operating limits can cause misconfiguration, but this is a definitional/administrative error of limited central relevance, capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Unit handling is largely manual with little automated dimensional checking, so ambiguous/mismatched bases are a chronic, poorly-served source of error. Reconciling units and reference bases across sources is tedious and slows configuration. Detecting ambiguous or mismatched units and reference bases demands careful expert scrutiny that few do consistently. Unit or reference-base errors can cause dosing errors that waste whole batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of exceeding the approved hold limit while awaiting intermediate vessel availability
rows.imp_rat: Exceeding hold limit while awaiting vessel availability (item 19) is a scheduling/queueing delay with low relevance to the batching process and modest, recoverable consequences. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Vessel-availability bottlenecks are a chronic, poorly buffered constraint, so hold-limit breaches while waiting remain common. The error is waiting time for a vessel exceeding the hold limit, driven by scheduling and equipment availability that plants struggle to compress. Avoiding the breach depends on schedulers and operators coordinating vessel turnover, a moderate planning skill. Batches held too long awaiting a vessel get downgraded or scrapped, and idle equipment adds cost, making this a recurring loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of interrupted agitation while the ingredient-addition sequence is active
rows.imp_rat: Interrupted agitation during ingredient addition can cause mixing inhomogeneity, but its transient nature and modest severity keep it peripheral to the batching CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Agitator-run interlocks tied to addition phases are common and reliable, though shorter unplanned agitation gaps still occur during upsets. The error is the duration agitation stays off during active addition, and automated interlocks that keep agitators running minimize that downtime well. Agitation-during-addition is usually enforced by interlock logic rather than operator attention, reducing skill dependence. Prolonged loss of agitation risks poor mixing and off-spec product, but interlocks keep such interruptions short and infrequent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that verification results are biased by out-of-tolerance measurement references
rows.imp_rat: Verification bias from out-of-tolerance references (item 5) is a metrology-support concern with low relevance to executing the batch and moderate, correctable consequences. Well under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Documented calibration and traceable references make out-of-tolerance reference bias uncommon, though drift between calibration intervals is a residual risk. Scheduled calibration is routine and does not disrupt normal batching time. Calibration programs offload the need for operators to judge reference accuracy. Calibration labor and standards are a predictable recurring cost far below the cost of biased releases.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of identifying the incorrect source of a batching-system fault
rows.imp_rat: Identifying the wrong source of a fault delays correct remediation, but fault diagnosis is a support activity of limited central relevance with moderate severity, ranked low.
rows.sat_rat: Diagnostics rely on technician reasoning and limited instrumentation, so misidentified fault sources are common and repeat repairs frequent. Fault diagnosis can consume extensive troubleshooting time when the true cause is elusive. Correctly diagnosing batching faults is deeply expertise-dependent and one of the hardest maintenance judgements. Misdiagnosis wastes labor and replaces good parts, a recurring and significant cost driver.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of additional batching-system components damaged during disassembly or repair
rows.imp_rat: Additional components damaged during disassembly or repair (defect) is a maintenance-collateral concern outside the core batching function, causing added cost and delay but not batch loss.
rows.sat_rat: Good procedures limit collateral damage, yet incidental damage to nearby components during disassembly remains a recurring occurrence. Careful disassembly slows work, and collateral damage triggers additional repair time. Avoiding collateral damage during teardown depends on technician care and familiarity with the equipment. Damaged adjacent components add unplanned parts and labor cost during service.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fluid leaking from the filter housing after replacement
rows.imp_rat: Fluid leaking from a filter housing after replacement (item 13) is a maintenance-reliability issue with low relevance to batching and modest, containable consequences. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Correct seal seating and pressure verification handle most cases, though improperly seated O-rings still cause occasional leaks. Filter changeouts and post-change leak checks are quick, standardized tasks. Proper seating of housing seals is a routine skill most maintenance techs perform reliably. Leaks waste product and require cleanup, but housings are cheap and reseating is fast.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that updated control software fails required compliance validation
rows.imp_rat: Control software failing compliance validation after an update (item 7) is an administrative/rework concern with low relevance to producing a batch and modest severity since it is caught before use. Held low.
rows.sat_rat: Established CSV and change-control frameworks make compliance failure at release uncommon when procedures are followed correctly. Compliance validation cycles are long and formalized but scheduled deliberately into revalidation plans. GMP/CSV validation specialists are required to interpret regulatory requirements, a well-defined but expert-dependent role. Documentation and validation labor is costly yet a standard line item in regulated maintenance budgets.

rows.stk: Instrumentation technicians, maintenance engineers, quality personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the required reference standard is outside its certification validity period when calibration begins
rows.imp_rat: An expired reference standard at calibration start is an administrative traceability lapse; it is peripheral to running a batch and easily caught, so both proxies are low.
rows.sat_rat: Calibration management systems that flag expired standards make using an out-of-cert reference rare where such systems are deployed. Checking standard certification status is a quick pre-calibration verification step. Verifying certification validity is a simple procedural check requiring little specialized skill. Recertifying standards is a routine, budgeted expense with no surprise cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaving pump or agitator components misaligned or improperly secured after reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned or improperly secured pump/agitator components after reassembly (item 14) is a service-quality defect with low relevance to routine batching and moderate mechanical consequences. Under the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Laser alignment tools and checklists help, but misalignment or under-torqued components remain a frequent latent defect after reassembly. Alignment and securing checks add time but are part of standard reassembly procedures. Precise shaft/coupling alignment and correct fastener securing depend on experienced technicians and can be error-prone under pressure. Misalignment causes premature bearing/seal wear and repeat repairs, driving avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of serviced pumps, valves, and connections that leak after reassembly
rows.imp_rat: Leaks after servicing pumps, valves, and connections (item 9) are a maintenance-quality issue with low relevance to the batching job itself and modest, typically contained consequences. Kept low.
rows.sat_rat: Post-service pressure/leak tests catch many leaks, but reassembly leaks remain a common recurring finding requiring rework. Reassembly and subsequent leak testing extends downtime, though pressure-test procedures are routine. Correct torqueing, gasket seating, and connection makeup rely heavily on technician craftsmanship and attention. Rework from post-assembly leaks wastes fluid, labor, and repeat testing time.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of active sterile fill-finish production disrupted by disconnecting shared services
rows.imp_rat: Disrupting active fill-finish by disconnecting shared services has moderate severity, but this scheduling/coordination delay is peripheral to executing the batching job and capped by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Lockout/isolation procedures reliably prevent cross-disruption when followed, though unexpected shared dependencies still surface. Disconnecting shared services during active production is the direct source of disruptive downtime that scheduling only partly contains. Planning safe isolation of shared utilities requires experienced facilities engineers. Lost sterile production hours during disconnection are costly but budgeted for planned shutdowns.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which residue or inaccessible surfaces obscure the physical condition of product-contact components
rows.imp_rat: Residue obscuring the condition of product-contact components hinders inspection with moderate severity, but this visibility error is peripheral to executing the batching job and ranked low.
rows.sat_rat: Residue and inaccessible geometry routinely hide true component condition, and no standard end-of-life inspection reliably overcomes this. Inspecting obscured product-contact surfaces requires slow disassembly and cleaning before condition can be judged. Interpreting residue-obscured surfaces for disposal decisions demands experienced inspectors. Extra teardown and borescope inspection to expose surfaces adds labor and tooling cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, system operators, batch technicians.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required recipe fields left blank before recipe release
rows.imp_rat: Required recipe fields left blank before release is a configuration-completeness gap with modest severity and limited centrality to the actual batching operation.
rows.sat_rat: Most modern recipe systems enforce mandatory-field checks at release, so this need is acceptably served though configuration gaps remain. Checking for blank required fields adds a review pass before release. Knowing which fields are truly required needs recipe-management familiarity but is fairly routine. Blank fields caught at release cost little; caught in production they cost batches.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the operator cannot access the pause controls because of the current control mode or access authority
rows.imp_rat: An operator unable to access pause controls due to mode or authority has safety relevance, but this access-permission error is peripheral to the core batching job and ranked below the top batch.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access and mode management are standard, but poorly configured authority levels occasionally leave the right operator unable to pause, a recurring edge case. When access is blocked by control mode or authority, resolving it costs time to switch modes or find authorized staff. The barrier is fundamentally about access rights and knowing the control-mode hierarchy, so it hinges on operator authorization and training. Inability to pause in time could allow a damaging excursion, but such lockout situations are relatively rare in well-configured systems.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of conflicting operator actions altering the same batch simultaneously
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting operator actions on the same batch can cause instability, but concurrent-edit control is a peripheral coordination error of limited central relevance, ranked low.
rows.sat_rat: Ownership/locking and access controls exist in many systems to prevent concurrent edits, but coordination gaps in multi-operator settings still let conflicting actions through. Conflicting simultaneous actions can cause rework or delays, but resolving them is usually quick once detected. Avoiding conflicts relies on operators coordinating verbally and following shift protocols, a coordination skill rather than a technical one. Conflicting changes to one batch can corrupt it and force disposition, an occasional but meaningful cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required process readings unavailable during endpoint verification
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable process readings during endpoint verification hamper confirmation, but missing data usually triggers a hold rather than causing harm, so both proxies stay low.
rows.sat_rat: Standard instrumentation covers most required readings, but sensor outages, fouling and gaps in coverage still leave some endpoint parameters unmeasured. Missing readings force waiting for manual samples, adding delay to endpoint verification. Working around an unavailable reading requires operator inference and experience. Backup lab analysis or extra sensors add moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration that process readings lag behind the batch's actual condition during endpoint verification
rows.imp_rat: Reading lag behind actual condition during endpoint verification can slightly misjudge completion, but this is a narrow timing issue with limited stakes for the core job.
rows.sat_rat: Inline sensing largely eliminates lag where installed, but many endpoints still rely on delayed lab confirmation that lags actual condition. The core error is measurement lag itself, and inline sensors keep it short while lab methods stretch it to minutes or longer. Compensating mentally for known lag is an experience-based skill. Faster inline analytics cost more but reduce off-spec at endpoint.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required equipment items that are faulted or unavailable at readiness confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Faulted/unavailable equipment at readiness is largely an availability/scheduling concern (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Equipment status monitoring flags many faults, though non-instrumented items still need manual verification. Faulted-equipment detection can be immediate via system status. Automated fault indication reduces operator diagnostic burden. Faulted equipment causes stoppages and loss, partly avoided by monitoring.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time awaiting required test results for recipe endpoint confirmation
rows.imp_rat: Time awaiting test results for endpoint confirmation is a delay concern (relevance); waiting is protective so consequences are limited (severity), low.
rows.sat_rat: Off-line test dependency means endpoint confirmation waits remain long and variable. Awaiting lab results for endpoint confirmation causes significant hold time. The delay is driven by lab turnaround, not operator skill. In-process hold while testing ties up capacity, a poorly contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between onset and recognition of an aseptic-condition excursion
rows.imp_rat: Delay recognizing an aseptic excursion has contamination implications, but the batch ceiling caps both proxies at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Environmental monitoring detection can lag onset, so aseptic excursion recognition remains slow and unreliable. Aseptic-condition excursions are often recognized late because monitoring may not be real-time at the point of impact. Recognizing subtle aseptic excursions depends on operator awareness and monitoring interpretation. Late recognition can compromise the whole batch, poorly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of conflicting records for the same compounding action
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting records for the same action creates ambiguity in the batch record, but it ranks low here and is held to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: A unified electronic record reduces conflicting entries, though parallel paper/side systems can still create duplicates. Single-system-of-record capture avoids reconciliation delay. One authoritative record removes operator effort reconciling duplicates. Conflicting records cause investigations, mostly prevented by single capture.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded parameter values lacking required units or execution context
rows.imp_rat: Missing units/execution context on recorded parameters weakens record interpretability, which touches the recording job, but the batch ceiling caps this below higher-priority integrity errors.
rows.sat_rat: Structured EBR fields reliably attach units and context to recorded values. Units and context are captured automatically with each value. Structured data entry removes operator burden of adding units and context. Missing context causes record rejection and rework, mostly avoided.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a critical utility interruption stopping an active compounding run
rows.imp_rat: Critical utility interruption stopping a run is disruptive, but ranked below prior items so both proxies are constrained to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Backup utilities and UPS reduce interruption-driven stoppages, but single-point failures still halt runs. Utility interruptions stop runs immediately with variable recovery time. Restoring utilities depends on facilities support rather than operator skill. A utility-caused stoppage can lose the in-process batch, a large cost partly mitigated.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a contamination-control breach stopping an active compounding run
rows.imp_rat: A contamination-control breach stopping a run is serious, but the batch ceiling forces relevance and severity down to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Contamination-control breaches remain a leading cause of run loss with limited system-level prevention. A contamination-control breach halts the run abruptly with lengthy recovery. Preventing breach-driven stoppages depends on aseptic discipline rather than tooling. A breach-triggered stop typically loses the batch, a costly and weakly contained risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time equipment remains outside the state required for the next recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Equipment lingering outside the required state for the next step is a delay concern (relevance) with recoverable impact (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Automated sequencing shortens state transitions for equipped systems, with manual steps remaining slower. Time to bring equipment into the required state depends on automated transitions. Achieving required state can require operator intervention where not automated. Equipment in wrong state idles production, partly contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of compounding run holds caused by parameter excursions
rows.imp_rat: Run holds caused by parameter excursions are a throughput/response concern (relevance); the hold is protective so consequences are limited (severity), low.
rows.sat_rat: Control systems limit excursion frequency, but holds still occur and their duration varies. Excursion-triggered holds interrupt runs and add recovery time. Clearing an excursion hold requires operator diagnosis and action. Holds idle equipment and risk material, partly contained by fast alarms.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of recipe holds pending required review or authorization
rows.imp_rat: Recipe holds pending review/authorization are a delay concern (relevance); the hold is protective so consequences are limited (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic review workflows exist but approver availability makes hold durations long and unpredictable. Holds pending review or authorization can be lengthy while approvers are found. The hold itself is procedural, but resolution depends on qualified reviewer availability. Review holds idle in-process batches, a poorly contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe steps blocked by incorrect operator entries or confirmations
rows.imp_rat: Steps blocked by incorrect operator entries cause rework (relevance) with minor, self-correcting consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Input validation catches some bad entries, but confirmation errors still block steps and need manual correction. Incorrect entries block progression and cost correction time. Correct confirmations depend on operator attention and understanding of prompts. Entry-blocked steps idle resources, a modest contained cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time recipe progression remains blocked by a mismatch between reported and actual equipment state
rows.imp_rat: Recipe progression blocked by reported/actual equipment-state mismatch is a delay concern (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Interlocks surface mismatches but resolution is manual, so blocking time varies widely. State-mismatch blocks stall progression until manually resolved. Diagnosing reported-vs-actual equipment mismatches requires troubleshooting skill. Blocked progression idles the line, a cost partly reduced by diagnostics.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time awaiting required conformance evidence before material use
rows.imp_rat: Time awaiting conformance evidence before material use is a delay concern (relevance); waiting is protective so impact is limited (severity), low.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-staged release evidence reduces waits, but late results still delay some material use. Waiting for conformance evidence before use adds real lead time. The wait is procedural rather than skill-dependent for the operator. Waiting idles staged materials and labor, partly contained by pre-release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material verifications delayed by conflicting requirements, lot records, or labels
rows.imp_rat: Material verifications delayed by conflicting requirements/labels is a workflow-ambiguity concern (relevance) with recoverable consequences (severity), low.
rows.sat_rat: Record and label discrepancies still require manual reconciliation, causing recurring delays. Verification delays from conflicting records/labels stall material use. Resolving conflicting lot records requires investigative judgement. Delayed verifications idle production, a weakly contained cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of doubt about the next safe action when contamination indications conflict
rows.imp_rat: Doubt about the next safe action during conflicting contamination signals matters, but ranking caps this at the 2.0 ceiling.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that equipment cleaning, sterilization, or line-clearance status fails recipe preconditions before execution
rows.imp_rat: Cleaning/line-clearance precondition failures carry contamination risk, but the batch ceiling forces both proxies down despite the inherent severity.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning and line-clearance preconditions still depend heavily on manual verification, leaving real failure risk. Line-clearance and cleaning status checks can delay start when done manually. Verifying cleaning and sterilization status often relies on operator sign-off and judgement. Cleaning status tracking is partly manual with limited system enforcement.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between completing a recipe step and confirming its completion
rows.imp_rat: Delay between completing a step and confirming it is a workflow-timing matter (relevance) with modest consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Confirmation delay is short where prompting is enabled, but operators still sometimes defer confirmations. Guided workflows prompt confirmation right after step completion. Prompted confirmation reduces reliance on operators to remember to close a step. Uses existing EBR workflow capability.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that affected compounding processing resumes before authorized continuation criteria are met
rows.imp_rat: Resuming processing before continuation criteria are met is a hold-enforcement risk, but the batch cap holds importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Authorized-resume gating is reliable where configured, but manual holds can be resumed prematurely. Resume authorization gates avoid premature restart without added delay. Continuation-criteria checks reduce reliance on operator judgement to resume. Configured hold-release logic within the MES.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from recognizing a hold-triggering condition until affected compounding processing is paused
rows.imp_rat: Delay pausing processing after a hold-triggering condition risks continued nonconformance, but the batch ceiling limits it to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Pausing on a hold is prompt for interlocked conditions but delayed where it depends on manual recognition. Hold triggers can pause processing quickly where interlocked, but manual holds lag. Recognizing a hold-triggering condition often depends on operator judgement. Automated hold interlocks require engineering investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an active execution hold is cleared during a control interruption or restart
rows.imp_rat: An execution hold cleared during a restart could allow unauthorized resumption, but ranked below prior items and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Holds can be inadvertently cleared during control interruptions or restarts, a known integrity risk. Restart routines aim to preserve hold state quickly after interruption. Hold-state persistence is a system property, not operator skill. Robust state persistence adds design cost not always fully realized.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that expected-condition limits for the active recipe are incorrect or unavailable
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect or unavailable expected-condition limits undermines enforcement, but the batch ordering places it at the 2.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Active-recipe limits are reliably available where version control governs, but stale or unavailable limits can occur. Recipe-bound limits load automatically with no start delay. System-supplied limits remove reliance on operator knowledge of ranges. Limit master data is configured once per recipe.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of short-lived parameter excursions that remain undetected between observations
rows.imp_rat: Undetected short-lived parameter excursions between observations can hide real departures, but the batch cap holds this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Short-lived excursions between observations commonly go undetected under periodic sampling. Sampling intervals leave gaps in which short excursions occur and pass undetected. Catching transient excursions between reads depends on monitoring density, not operator skill. Higher-frequency sensing to close gaps carries added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that captured parameter or step evidence is altered or lost before deviation review
rows.imp_rat: Captured evidence altered or lost before deviation review is a data-integrity risk, but ranked below prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Captured evidence is well protected from alteration or loss by audit trails, though pre-commit windows carry slight risk. Immediate commit and audit logging protect evidence before review. Data integrity is system-enforced, not operator-dependent. Uses validated storage already in place.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of deviation records altered after initial entry without attributable authorization
rows.imp_rat: Deviation records altered without attributable authorization is a data-integrity concern, but ranking caps importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Deviation record changes are reliably attributed through immutable audit trails. Audit trails capture changes instantly with no added operator effort. Automated audit logging removes reliance on operators recording who changed what. Audit trail is a built-in validated-system feature.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of starting a recipe step before prior-step results and required conditions are satisfied
rows.imp_rat: Starting a step before prior-step results/conditions are satisfied undermines sequence enforcement, but is capped at 2.0 by the ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Prior-result gating is reliable where dependencies are configured, though not all conditions are interlocked. Prior-step gating blocks premature starts without added delay. Interlocks reduce reliance on operators to verify prior results. Configured in recipe step dependencies.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of compromising required sterile conditions while executing an open-processing recipe step
rows.imp_rat: Compromising sterile conditions during an open-processing step is a contamination risk, but the batch ceiling holds proxies at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Sterility during open processing is governed by manual aseptic practice, an inherently variable and unmitigated risk. Open-processing steps have no system control over the moment sterility is at risk. Preserving sterile conditions during open steps rests entirely on operator technique. Assurance comes from aseptic training and gowning, not system cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that acceptance limits from the wrong recipe revision govern the compounding run
rows.imp_rat: Wrong-revision acceptance limits governing a run undermines enforcement correctness, but the batch cap holds both proxies at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Wrong-revision limits are uncommon where MES revision control is enforced. Version-controlled limits load with the released recipe instantly. Automated versioning removes operator involvement in selecting limits. Built into recipe version management.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter setpoint changes made without required authorization during the compounding run
rows.imp_rat: Setpoint changes made without authorization during a run undermines enforcement control, but the batch cap holds proxies at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Unauthorized setpoint changes are uncommon where role-based access and e-signatures are enforced. Authorization prompts gate setpoint changes without meaningful delay. Access control removes reliance on operators policing who may change setpoints. Role-based access is standard configuration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that reconciliation uses a recipe revision other than the one authorized for the compounding batch
rows.imp_rat: Reconciliation using an unauthorized revision is a genuine control defect, yet ranked below the prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Reconciliation generally uses the authorized revision where version control governs, with rare manual override risk. Reconciliation against the authorized revision is system-driven and prompt. Automated version binding reduces reliance on operator selection. Part of recipe management configuration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recorded parameters conflicting with prescribed values or limits without disposition
rows.imp_rat: Recorded parameters conflicting with limits without disposition weakens enforcement, but the batch ordering caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Values conflicting with limits are flagged but often remain without prompt disposition until review. Out-of-limit flags appear in real time, but disposition follows later. Deciding disposition for conflicting recorded values relies on operator/QA judgement. Deviation disposition is largely manual labor.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between a material addition and its recording against the recipe requirement
rows.imp_rat: Latency between a material addition and its recording is a contemporaneity concern (relevance) with limited harm if eventually recorded (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Automated capture keeps the addition-to-record delay short and consistent where dispensing is integrated. Integrated dispensing records additions against the recipe requirement almost immediately. Automated linkage removes operator effort in matching addition to requirement. Relies on already-installed dispensing integration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of parameter records changed without attributable authorization
rows.imp_rat: Parameter records changed without attributable authorization undermines record integrity, but the batch order limits it to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Parameter changes without attributable authorization are rare given enforced audit trails and e-signatures. Changes are logged in real time by the audit trail. No operator effort needed for attribution. Standard part- capability, already owned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a process alarm activates after the affected condition has compromised the compounding run
rows.imp_rat: A late alarm activating after the condition compromised the run undermines timely enforcement, but the batch cap limits it to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Late-activating alarms after compromise remain a real limitation of threshold-based alarming. Some alarms fire only after the condition has already affected the batch, too late to act. Anticipating late alarms depends on operator experience with the process. Predictive alarming requires added engineering and cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of unavailable critical alarm indication during an active compounding run
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable critical alarm indication during an active run is a real risk, but ranked below prior items so proxies stay at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Critical alarm availability is high but not guaranteed during control interruptions. Critical alarm indication is typically continuous with minimal downtime. Availability is a system property independent of operator skill. Alarm redundancy requires investment not uniformly present.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of prioritizing a less consequential alarm over one approaching batch compromise
rows.imp_rat: Prioritizing a less consequential alarm over one approaching batch compromise risks missing a critical condition, but capped at 2.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Prioritizing a lesser alarm over one nearing batch compromise remains a recurring risk under poor rationalization. Poor prioritization wastes time on lesser alarms while a critical one waits. Correctly ranking alarm consequence relies on operator judgement, especially in alarm floods. Alarm rationalization projects add cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that responding to a process alarm compromises required sterile conditions
rows.imp_rat: An alarm response compromising sterile conditions is a genuine contamination risk, but the batch cap limits importance to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Responding to alarms without compromising sterility relies almost entirely on manual practice, a persistent vulnerability. Rapid alarm response can pressure operators into actions that break sterile conditions. Maintaining sterility while responding demands high operator discipline and training. No systemic control; cost is in training and procedure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a quarantined or unreleased material lot is cleared for addition to the sterile compounding batch
rows.imp_rat: Clearing a quarantined lot into a sterile batch is a serious control failure, but the batch ceiling caps its importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Quarantine/hold status enforcement reliably prevents unreleased-lot use where integration is complete. Quarantine status blocks clearance immediately at addition. Automated status checks remove reliance on operators knowing lot status. Uses existing ERP/QC status integration.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a material lot is cleared after its expiration or retest limit
rows.imp_rat: Clearing an expired lot affects material conformance, but it ranks below prior items so proxies stay at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Expiry and retest enforcement is highly reliable where lot dates are in the system. Expiry checks are instantaneous at scan or clearance. Automated date checks remove reliance on operators reading expiry dates. Uses existing material master and scanning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material outside recipe-defined assay, potency, or concentration limits is cleared for addition
rows.imp_rat: Clearing material outside assay/potency limits risks product quality, but ranking constraints hold this error at the 2.0 maximum.
rows.sat_rat: Release-linked checks reliably block out-of-spec lots where QC data is integrated, with gaps for manual dispositions. Release-status lookups block out-of-spec material quickly at addition. Automated assay/release checks reduce reliance on operator interpretation of CoA. Uses existing QC and release data linkage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material with an unacceptable microbial or endotoxin result is cleared for addition
rows.imp_rat: Clearing material with unacceptable microbial/endotoxin result is a genuine contamination risk, yet capped by the batch ordering at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Release-status enforcement reliably blocks failing lots where QC integration exists. Microbial/endotoxin release status is checked at clearance without added delay. Automated release gating removes reliance on operator interpretation of microbial results. Relies on integrated QC release status.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compromised material-container integrity remains undetected before addition
rows.imp_rat: Undetected compromised container integrity is a contamination concern, but ranking places it here at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Compromised container integrity frequently goes undetected because it depends on manual visual checks with no system backstop. Container integrity is judged by manual visual inspection, which adds no system delay but no assurance either. Detecting compromised integrity relies almost entirely on operator observation and training. No automated tooling; cost is in operator time only.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material temperature is outside recipe-defined limits when conformance is checked
rows.imp_rat: Material temperature outside limits at check time affects conformance, yet it ranks below the previous batch and is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Instrumented temperature checks are reliable, but manually measured materials still risk undetected excursions. Temperature sensing gives immediate conformance readings where instrumented. Automated temperature checks reduce reliance on manual measurement. Uses existing temperature instrumentation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that material exceeds its recipe-defined hold or exposure allowance before addition
rows.imp_rat: Material exceeding hold/exposure allowance is a real contamination-risk error, but the batch ordering caps its importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Hold and exposure limits are often tracked manually, so exceedances before addition remain a real risk. Hold/exposure timers, where tracked, alert before limits are exceeded. Manual hold tracking depends on operators calculating exposure times. Automated hold-time tracking is inconsistently implemented.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of material additions begun before recipe-defined use conditions are confirmed
rows.imp_rat: Additions begun before use conditions confirmed risks conformance, but ranking constraints keep this error at or below the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where use conditions are interlocked additions are held reliably, but not all conditions are systematized. Precondition gating can block early additions without added delay. Automated use-condition checks reduce reliance on operator confirmation. Configured within recipe preconditions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of compounding batches that start after their planned start
rows.imp_rat: Batches starting after their planned start is a scheduling concern outside the core enforce/record job (relevance) with minimal harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Start times slip routinely due to upstream dependencies, so this need is served only modestly. Late batch starts are a direct time loss that the status quo struggles to prevent under real conditions. Hitting planned starts depends on the overseer coordinating people and materials, a demanding orchestration. Late starts erode capacity utilization, a throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compounding batches executed outside the planned sequence
rows.imp_rat: Batches executed outside planned sequence is chiefly a scheduling/instability concern (relevance) with limited direct quality harm (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: MES scheduling constrains order reasonably well, though manual overrides during disruptions still cause deviations. Sequence enforcement in scheduling keeps out-of-order execution from consuming rework time. Scheduling tools reduce the overseer's burden to remember the planned order. Out-of-sequence batches can waste setup and material, a moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount by which compounding cycle length exceeds the scheduled window
rows.imp_rat: Cycle length exceeding the scheduled window is a throughput matter largely outside the core enforce/record job (relevance) with low harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Cycle length varies with disruptions and manual handling, so overruns recur predictably. Cycle overruns are the direct time error and the status quo offers limited real-time levers to contain them. Keeping cycles within window relies on experienced pacing and intervention judgement. Overruns eat capacity and can delay downstream filling, a throughput cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between the onset of compounding schedule slippage and recognition by the shift overseer
rows.imp_rat: Delayed recognition of schedule slippage is a productivity concern only tangential to recipe enforcement (relevance) with modest consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Few systems surface schedule-adherence trends live, so slippage recognition is routinely late. Schedule slippage is usually noticed only when it has already accumulated, a slow-feedback delay. Spotting early slippage requires the overseer to mentally track plan-versus-actual, a demanding manual task. Late recognition compresses the rest of the shift and can force overtime, a cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in the priority of concurrent disruptions affecting product quality or shift output
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in prioritizing concurrent disruptions is a coordination/decision issue only loosely tied to recipe enforcement (relevance) with modest impact (severity).
rows.sat_rat: No standard decision support exists, so priority calls are inconsistent and ambiguous under load. Deciding priority among concurrent disruptions costs precious minutes when several fire at once. Ranking simultaneous quality-versus-output disruptions demands seasoned judgement that is scarce and highly variable between overseers. Mis-prioritization can sacrifice product quality or shift output, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of compounding downtime before an authorized controlled restart
rows.imp_rat: Downtime before an authorized controlled restart is an availability concern (relevance); the controlled restart is protective, so consequences are limited (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Restart authorization processes are manual and sequential, so downtime frequently stretches longer than necessary. Downtime before an authorized restart is often prolonged by approval and diagnosis delays. A controlled restart needs qualified sign-off and judgement that may not be immediately available. Extended downtime idles equipment and staff, a clear cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of misclassifying a recording anomaly as a compounding deviation or vice versa
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying recording anomaly vs deviation affects investigation accuracy, but it ranks here at the capped 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Classification relies on human judgement with little system support, so misclassification is a persistent risk. Correctly classifying an anomaly can require prolonged review before disposition. Telling a recording error apart from a real deviation is highly expertise-dependent. Misclassification wastes investigation effort or lets real defects pass.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of investigations closed without an evidence-supported causal conclusion
rows.imp_rat: Investigations closed without a causal conclusion weakens quality assurance, but ranking places this at the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Many investigations still close without robust causal evidence despite CAPA processes, so quality of closure varies. Reaching an evidence-supported cause takes considerable investigation time. Root-cause conclusions depend heavily on skilled investigators and are hard to guarantee. Weak causal closure leads to recurrence and repeated remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that aggregation of quality indicators masks a batch-level process excursion
rows.imp_rat: Aggregation masking a batch-level excursion could hide a real deviation, but ranked lower here so both proxies are held to the 2.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Aggregated reporting routinely obscures batch-level excursions and standard practice offers weak safeguards against it. Detecting masking requires additional drill-down analysis that adds time. Recognizing when aggregation hides a batch excursion needs strong statistical skill. Masked excursions escape to product, risking costly downstream failures.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of indicator breaches that do not correspond to a quality requirement violation
rows.imp_rat: Indicator breaches not corresponding to real violations are false alarms (relevance) with nuisance-level consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm thresholds are often poorly tuned, so nuisance breaches unrelated to requirement violations are frequent. Chasing false indicator breaches consumes investigation time promptly triggered by each alert. Distinguishing true violations from noise depends on experienced judgement. False breaches waste investigation resources without quality benefit.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of challenged recipe alarms and interlocks that fail to produce the intended response
rows.imp_rat: Challenged alarms/interlocks failing to respond is a safety-enforcement risk, but ranked below prior batch and capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm and interlock testing is a well-established practice with generally dependable results when performed. Challenging every alarm and interlock is time-consuming to execute case by case. Provoking and verifying interlock responses requires skilled test engineering. Alarm/interlock testing is standard scope with predictable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of test deviations unresolved when the acceptance recommendation is due
rows.imp_rat: Test deviations unresolved when a recommendation is due is a workflow-timing concern peripheral to executing recipes (relevance) with limited harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Tracking tools make open items visible, yet a residue of unresolved deviations at recommendation time is common. Deviations often remain open right up to the acceptance deadline because closure competes with schedule. Resolving test deviations correctly requires skilled root-cause analysis that bottlenecks on a few experts. Carrying open deviations to the decision point risks costly conditional acceptances or reopenings.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of evidence items lacking traceability to acceptance criteria and evaluated recipe behavior
rows.imp_rat: Evidence items lacking traceability to acceptance criteria is a documentation-linkage concern (relevance) with limited operational harm (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Traceability matrices are standard practice, yet untraced evidence items remain a frequent finding. Establishing traceability from each evidence item to criteria is time-consuming manual linkage work. Maintaining coherent traceability requires disciplined expertise that many teams lack. Broken traceability is inexpensive to create but expensive to reconstruct under audit scrutiny.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recorded compounding values that differ from the values applied during execution
rows.imp_rat: Recorded values differing from applied values corrupts the process record's accuracy, but the batch ceiling holds this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Automated recording in MES generally keeps recorded values faithful to applied values, a strength of the status quo. Comparing recorded versus applied values requires deliberate verification time. Detecting record-versus-execution divergence needs attentive technical review. Automated data capture keeps this verification relatively low-cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compounding record is altered outside authorized controls without detection
rows.imp_rat: Undetected unauthorized record alteration is a data-integrity concern, but ranked below the prior batch so proxies stay at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails and access controls in modern MES make undetected unauthorized record alteration comparatively well guarded. Verifying tamper-detection controls adds targeted test time. Evaluating audit-trail and access controls requires data-integrity expertise. Robust tamper controls are built into modern systems at manageable incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the rate of record corruption or loss during transfers between recipe execution, storage, review, and archival stages
rows.imp_rat: Record corruption/loss during transfers between stages threatens data integrity, but ranked below prior items so proxies stay at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checksums and interface validation reduce corruption, but transfer-stage record loss remains an occasional real risk. Testing transfer integrity across stages adds verification time. Validating data transfers requires interface and integration expertise. Transfer verification adds moderate cost but tooling exists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of record changes lacking actor, timestamp, prior-value, or reason attribution
rows.imp_rat: Record changes lacking actor/timestamp/prior-value attribution is an audit-trail defect, but capped by ranking at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Part -compliant audit trails reliably capture actor, timestamp, prior value and reason in mature systems. Verifying full attribution on record changes adds review time. Confirming complete audit-trail attributes requires data-integrity literacy. Modern audit trails capture attribution automatically at low marginal cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that restored compounding records differ from their pre-recovery state
rows.imp_rat: Restored records differing from pre-recovery state threatens record integrity, but the batch ceiling limits both proxies to 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Backup/restore validation is established practice and restored records generally match pre-recovery state. Verifying restore fidelity requires backup/restore test cycles that take time. Backup and recovery validation needs IT and data-integrity skills. Backup infrastructure and restore testing carry manageable cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an inapplicable data-integrity requirement is used to adjudicate a discrepancy
rows.imp_rat: Using an inapplicable data-integrity requirement to adjudicate a discrepancy is a niche adjudication error (relevance) with contained consequences (severity), low.
rows.sat_rat: Applicability judgements are inconsistent, so inapplicable requirements are sometimes used to adjudicate. Determining which requirement applies to a discrepancy slows adjudication when scope is unclear. Applying the correct requirement demands regulatory expertise that is scarce and uneven. Misapplied requirements cause wrong dispositions with real remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unauthorized recipe changes not identified during adjudication
rows.imp_rat: Unauthorized recipe changes missed at adjudication undermine enforcement, yet the batch ordering places this below prior items, capping importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails exist but unauthorized changes can still evade detection during adjudication. Detecting unauthorized changes during adjudication requires time to review change histories. Interpreting change logs to catch unauthorized edits demands trained review. Missed unauthorized changes create expensive integrity investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of compliant recipe or parameter differences incorrectly classified as integrity failures
rows.imp_rat: Compliant differences misclassified as integrity failures cause false positives (relevance) with mainly nuisance/rework consequences (severity), scored low.
rows.sat_rat: Without clear decision rules, benign differences are sometimes misclassified as failures. Distinguishing compliant differences from real failures adds review time as each case is examined. Correct classification depends heavily on expert interpretation of what constitutes an integrity failure. False-positive failures trigger needless investigations that waste resources.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of unresolved conflicts among system records, governing procedures, and stakeholder interpretations used in the recommendation
rows.imp_rat: Unresolved conflicts among records/procedures/interpretations in the recommendation is a decision-clarity concern (relevance) with contained consequences (severity).
rows.sat_rat: Conflicts frequently remain unresolved in the recommendation because there is no systematic reconciliation. Reconciling conflicting records, procedures and interpretations extends the review timeline substantially. Resolving these conflicts is expert-intensive and depends on senior arbitration. Unresolved conflicts lead to rework and disputed dispositions with cost implications.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that alterations to compounding records lack attributable traces
rows.imp_rat: Untraceable alterations to records weaken data integrity, yet the ranking places this error below the prior batch, capping importance at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Modern GMP audit trails make untraceable record alterations rare where systems are validated. Audit-trail review to confirm attributability is routine and reasonably quick in validated systems. System audit trails reduce the skill needed to establish who changed what. Attributable traces are largely built-in, avoiding costly forensic reconstruction.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe versions lacking substantiated reviews and approvals
rows.imp_rat: Recipe versions lacking substantiated reviews touches enforcement quality, but per batch ranking it sits below the higher-scored approval controls and is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic approval workflows make unapproved versions in production fairly uncommon. Confirming that each recipe version has substantiated reviews is a moderate documentation check. System-enforced approval workflows lower the expertise needed to verify approvals exist. Missing approvals cause costly requalification, but workflows largely prevent this.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that compounding records become unavailable before retention obligations expire
rows.imp_rat: Records becoming unavailable before retention obligations expire is a compliance-integrity risk, but ranked here at the 2.0 cap.
rows.sat_rat: Validated records-retention and backup regimes make premature record loss rare. Retention is managed automatically, so no per-record time burden arises. Validated archiving reduces reliance on staff to preserve records. Automated retention avoids costly loss and reconstruction.

rows.stk: Process engineers, procurement leads, project managers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of overlooking a required batch-size or operating-range mismatch during evaluation
rows.imp_rat: Overlooking a batch-size or operating-range mismatch during evaluation is an assessment-defect step upstream of batching (relevance low); consequences bounded by the batch-3 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Operating-range mismatches are sometimes overlooked in evaluation because they hide in detailed specifications. Catching batch-size or operating-range mismatches during evaluation requires deliberate cross-checking that adds review time. Recognizing subtle operating-range mismatches depends on an experienced evaluator's attention to detail. Overlooked mismatches lead to expensive rework, while the evaluation check is low-cost.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of wetted components whose material identity is unverified
rows.imp_rat: Unverified wetted-component material identity risks compatibility and contamination issues (relevance) with quality consequences (severity), but the ceiling keeps it at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Material certificates cover many components but positive verification of every wetted part is inconsistently done, leaving unverified items. Verifying material identity via mill certs and testing adds documentation and lab time. Confirming actual wetted-component material identity needs metallurgical or PMI expertise beyond routine review. Material testing and traceability add cost, and unverified materials risk expensive failures.

rows.stk: Process engineers, materials engineers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of intended fluid chemistry and process requirements missing from the compatibility evaluation basis
rows.imp_rat: Missing fluid chemistry and process requirements from the compatibility basis can lead to material failures (relevance) and contamination (severity), but this batch is capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Compatibility evaluation bases are frequently incomplete because some process conditions get overlooked, a recurring reliability gap. Compiling all fluid chemistry and process requirements into the compatibility basis is an upfront effort that adds time. Knowing which chemistry and process conditions must be captured requires experienced process and materials specialists. Missing requirements in the basis cause costly compatibility failures, though compilation cost is modest.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that submitted certifications are expired, invalid, or outside the proposed batching system's scope
rows.imp_rat: Expired or out-of-scope certifications are a procurement/compliance-vetting error only loosely connected to running a batch (low relevance) and correctable before use (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Certificate expiry and scope checks are well-defined and reliably performed, though scope-mismatch subtleties occasionally slip by. Verifying certification validity and scope is a fast lookup against issuing bodies. Checking certificate currency and scope requires only modest training once the requirements are known. Certification verification is cheap and prevents costly acceptance of invalid credentials.

rows.stk: Procurement leads, quality engineers, regulatory affairs personnel.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between compliance evidence and the proposed batching system configuration
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between compliance evidence and system configuration is a documentation-alignment issue rather than a batching-function failure (low relevance), reconcilable without harm (low severity). Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Evidence often pertains to similar-but-not-identical configurations, and this mismatch is only partially caught in practice. Matching compliance evidence to the exact proposed configuration adds a reconciliation step to review. Detecting that evidence covers a different configuration than proposed takes an attentive, knowledgeable reviewer. Configuration-evidence mismatches cause costly requalification, but checking is inexpensive.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical sterile fill-finish requirements omitted from the requirements baseline
rows.imp_rat: Missing sterile requirements in the baseline is a documentation error with downstream but indirect impact on the batching CFJ (low relevance) and manageable consequences (low severity). Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: URS templates and regulatory checklists exist, but incomplete baselines remain a common root cause of downstream problems. Building a complete requirements baseline is an upfront effort that adds time before RFQ release. Assembling a complete critical-requirement set relies heavily on experienced engineers and quality staff to recall all applicable sterile requirements. Baseline development is mostly staff time, but omitted requirements drive costly late redesign.

rows.stk: Process engineers, project managers, procurement leads.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between specified batching capacity and projected production demand
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between batching capacity and projected demand is a planning-level defect that affects throughput but not the core act of preparing a batch correctly (relevance low); severity is bounded and ranked below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Demand-to-capacity sizing is well-established practice, though uncertain demand forecasts still leave residual mismatch risk. Capacity sizing against demand projections is a routine calculation that adds little time. Matching batching capacity to demand uses standard process-engineering sizing methods that most engineers handle. Mismatched capacity wastes capital or forces re-procurement, but the analysis itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of fluid leaking from inlet, outlet, or recirculation piping joints during transfer
rows.imp_rat: Fluid leaking from piping joints during transfer touches batch delivery, yet the ranking places it below the prior batch so relevance and severity are both held at 2.
rows.sat_rat: Proven jointing methods and post-assembly testing make transfer-path leakage uncommon in mature installations. Joint leaks during transfer cause interruptions, but well-made connections rarely require in-service repair. Assembling leak-tight inlet, outlet, and recirculation joints is standard competent-fitter work with defined torque and gasket practice. In-service joint leaks waste process fluid and cleanup effort, though these are infrequent after testing.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of import delay caused by source records that cannot be interpreted
rows.imp_rat: Import delay from uninterpretable source records is peripheral to physically batching and preparing liquids (low relevance), and its consequences are a recoverable data-ingest slowdown (low severity). Capped at 2.0 per batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Import tooling handles clean records well but has weak tolerance for malformed inputs, so unparseable records reliably trigger delay. Uninterpretable source records force manual re-keying or clarification loops that directly extend import duration. Reconstructing meaning from malformed source data relies on people who know the legacy formats, a dependency that slows things when they are unavailable. Import delays consume labor but rarely destroy material, keeping direct cost moderate.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of assigning recipe-editing permissions beyond approved responsibilities
rows.imp_rat: Over-broad recipe-editing permissions is an access-control defect with some relevance to recipe integrity and moderate severity, but ranked below batch 2. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Least-privilege intent is widely accepted but in practice permissions are often granted generously to avoid rework, so scope creep on recipe editing remains a recurring gap. Restricting recipe-editing rights is a configuration step that adds little time when role templates exist. Deciding who legitimately needs recipe-edit authority requires governance judgement that is often delegated to overburdened administrators without full context. Over-broad edit rights create latent compliance and product-quality exposure that is expensive if exploited, though direct cost at setup is negligible.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch-start delay when product-contact surfaces do not meet pre-use acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Batch-start delay when contact surfaces fail pre-use criteria protects against contamination (relevance) with hygiene consequences (severity), but the ranking caps this at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning validation and pre-use inspection are mature practices, so most surfaces pass, but borderline results still trigger recurring holds. Batch start is often held while surfaces are re-cleaned or re-swabbed to meet acceptance criteria, a common and disruptive delay. Interpreting whether surfaces pass pre-use criteria relies on trained inspectors and defined cleaning validation, which is reasonably established. Re-cleaning consumes solvents, water, labor, and delayed throughput when surfaces fail initial checks.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of leaks or pressure-loss defects remaining undetected after integrity testing
rows.imp_rat: Leaks remaining undetected after integrity testing bear on batch containment, but under the ceiling both relevance and severity are held to 2 as this item ranks below the previous batch.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure and leak testing are mature and catch most defects, but slow or intermittent leaks can evade standard test durations. Integrity testing itself is time-bounded, but undetected leaks surface later and trigger far larger schedule loss. Interpreting pressure-decay and detecting slow leaks requires trained technicians and careful method. Undetected defects escaping to operation cause expensive batch loss and downtime later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of assembled connections and containment sections omitted from integrity testing
rows.imp_rat: Connections omitted from integrity testing is a verification-coverage error ranked below the prior batch; relevance is modest and severity capped since it represents a process gap rather than a realized failure.
rows.sat_rat: Test packages and punch lists cover most connections, but omissions from complex systems still occur without a robust closure system. Tracking which connections were tested versus omitted takes coordination time, and gaps found late force re-testing. Complete test coverage depends on diligent tracking and inspector experience rather than automatic verification. Untested sections that later fail impose costly late rework and re-cleaning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of liquid-contact surfaces or open process connections becoming contaminated during positioning and mounting
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of liquid-contact surfaces during positioning bears on batch purity, but the ranking ceiling holds relevance and severity to 2 as this item sits below the prior batch.
rows.sat_rat: Protective caps and clean-handling protocols help, but exposure during positioning still causes recurring contamination events. Contamination during positioning forces re-cleaning that delays assembly progress. Keeping contact surfaces protected during mounting demands disciplined handling that depends on trained, attentive crews. Re-cleaning contaminated surfaces consumes cleaning agents, labor, and delays.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percent of the prepared batch remaining in the source vessel after transfer
rows.imp_rat: Prepared batch remaining in the source vessel after transfer is a yield defect ranked below the prior batch; relevance and severity both stay at 2 as it concerns recoverable material rather than the core job's success.
rows.sat_rat: Transfer completeness is reasonably repeatable but clingy or high-viscosity products leave inconsistent amounts behind. Chasing the last of a transfer can extend pump-out time modestly. Achieving full transfer depends mostly on vessel and pump design, not operator finesse. Residual product left in the source vessel is a direct recurring yield loss, partly mitigated by conical bottoms and scraped surfaces.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of loss of liquid containment while independently actuating system functions
rows.imp_rat: Loss of containment while independently actuating functions is a commissioning/test-time event, peripheral to core batching (low relevance) with limited severity in that context. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Mature interlock logic, double-block-and-bleed valving and pressure/level permissives make containment loss during independent actuation an uncommon event in well-maintained systems. Interlock-mediated actuation adds minor sequencing delays but does not materially slow the operator's independent function control. Modern PLC-driven valve sequencing and permissive logic remove much of the manual judgement once needed to actuate functions without spilling. Containment failures during actuation are costly in lost product and cleanup, but engineered valve interlocks keep such events rare.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of batch material discarded after an incorrect recipe change
rows.imp_rat: Batch material discarded after an incorrect recipe change is a waste consequence with some batching relevance and moderate severity, but placed in the lower tier. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Recipe management systems with version control reduce errors, but confirmation gaps and manual overrides still let incorrect recipes reach production. Discovering an incorrect recipe change mid-batch forces a stop that consumes time before material can be salvaged or dumped. Preventing bad recipe changes depends on operator diligence and change-control discipline that varies widely by site. A wrong recipe change typically means the whole batch is scrapped, a large and direct material loss that current safeguards only partly prevent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recipe ingredient pathways not exercised during accuracy verification
rows.imp_rat: Untested ingredient pathways during accuracy verification is a verification-coverage defect; it affects assurance rather than the batching job directly (relevance low) and severity is bounded by the ranking below batch 2.
rows.sat_rat: Accuracy verification commonly samples representative paths rather than all of them, leaving untested pathways as a latent, poorly addressed reliability gap. Verifying every ingredient pathway extends validation testing time considerably. Designing coverage that exercises all pathways demands validation expertise that many operating teams lack. Unexercised pathways rarely cost money directly until an unverified route fails in production.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile fill-finish area during functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced during functional testing is a test-phase risk peripheral to routine batching (low relevance); severity constrained here despite contamination stakes. Held to ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic protocols and environmental monitoring control most risk, but any intrusion into the fill-finish zone carries real, hard-to-eliminate contamination risk. Aseptic gowning, cleaning, and re-sanitization around testing add substantial time. Maintaining sterility during functional testing demands rigorous aseptic technique and constant vigilance from trained staff. A contamination breach can scrap batches and force costly line re-sterilization.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish activity caused by functional testing
rows.imp_rat: Unplanned interruption to sterile fill-finish from functional testing is a scheduling disruption peripheral to core batching (low relevance) with recoverable severity. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduled maintenance windows and staged testing reduce disruption, but unplanned test-driven interruptions to sterile activity still recur. Functional testing that touches sterile fill-finish forces line stoppages that are hard to fully overlap with production. Coordinating testing to minimize interruption requires planning expertise but not rare technical skill. Lost sterile production time during interruptions is expensive per hour.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production unavailability beyond the maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged sterile fill-finish unavailability beyond the maintenance window is downstream of batching and only moderately central to the batching CFJ (relevance low); consequences can be real but the ranking places this below batch 2, capping severity.
rows.sat_rat: Return-to-service timing is unpredictable because unexpected findings during maintenance frequently extend the outage. Overruns of production unavailability are common because re-qualification and cleaning after maintenance take longer than planned. Restoring sterile production quickly hinges on skilled technicians and QA staff whose availability is limited. Each extra hour of sterile fill-finish downtime carries very high batch-value cost that current practice poorly contains.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required batching-system maintenance tasks omitted from the maintenance schedule
rows.imp_rat: Omitting required maintenance tasks from the schedule is an administrative planning defect with limited centrality to the immediate batching job; relevance and severity both stay low as consequences are deferred and correctable.
rows.sat_rat: CMMS-driven PM schedules capture most routine tasks, though gaps for less common components still recur. Omitted tasks eventually surface as unplanned work that consumes time later. Building a complete task list depends on planner knowledge of every component's requirements. Missed tasks lead to premature failures and rework costs that a complete schedule would avoid.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that maintenance due dates are calculated from incomplete or inaccurate operating and condition data
rows.imp_rat: Calculating maintenance due dates from incomplete condition data is an administrative reliability error, tangential to the core batching job; relevance is low and severity mild since it affects scheduling accuracy rather than immediate batch quality.
rows.sat_rat: Many systems still rely on calendar intervals with incomplete runtime/condition inputs, so due-date accuracy is frequently compromised. Due dates computed from stale data can trigger premature or late work, disrupting timing. Judging whether underlying condition data is complete requires expertise that few maintenance planners apply consistently. Bad due-date math causes either over-maintenance or failure-driven repairs, both costly.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching-system unavailability beyond the scheduled maintenance window
rows.imp_rat: Batching-system downtime beyond the scheduled window affects availability but is an uptime concern rather than the accuracy of preparation itself (relevance low); severity constrained by the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard work packages and spares kitting help, but overrun frequency remains a persistent, only partly mitigated issue. Maintenance overruns beyond the scheduled window happen regularly when parts or unexpected faults appear mid-job. Bringing the batching system back on time depends on experienced maintainers who can diagnose and correct quickly. Extended outages consume overtime labor and lost production capacity that current practice only partly limits.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical diagnostic alerts go unnoticed during production
rows.imp_rat: Unnoticed diagnostic alerts touch on reliability, but as an alert-monitoring concern it is somewhat removed from the core preparation job (relevance low) and severity is bounded by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm systems exist and are configured, but alarm flooding and inconsistent acknowledgment mean critical alerts still slip through. Alerts surface in near real time on SCADA, but operator response can lag during busy production. Noticing and correctly triaging critical alerts depends heavily on attentive, trained operators amid many competing signals. Missed critical alerts can cause expensive damage, though the alerting infrastructure itself is low cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of false diagnostic alerts that obscure actionable batching system faults
rows.imp_rat: False diagnostic alerts obscuring real faults is a reliability/noise defect somewhat removed from the preparation job (relevance low); consequences bounded under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Alarm rationalization is known practice but poorly implemented at many sites, so false alerts commonly obscure real faults. False alerts waste responder time chasing non-issues during otherwise productive periods. Distinguishing genuine faults from nuisance alarms requires experienced judgement that varies by operator. Nuisance alarms drive unnecessary inspection labor and desensitize staff at ongoing cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of process trends being misinterpreted as normal equipment behavior
rows.imp_rat: Misinterpreting process trends as normal is a diagnostic-ambiguity error at the margin of the core job (low relevance), and mistakes are usually caught downstream (low severity). Capped per batch rule.
rows.sat_rat: Historian trending exists but interpretation is manual and inconsistent, so normal-looking drift is routinely missed. Trend drift is slow and easily unnoticed until a failure forces attention, wasting lead time. Correctly reading process trends as early degradation demands scarce analytical expertise rarely present at the operator level. Misread trends lead to late interventions and higher repair cost, but trend tools themselves are cheap.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of decommissioned system materials assigned incorrect disposal classifications
rows.imp_rat: Incorrect disposal classification of decommissioned materials is an end-of-life compliance task; it barely touches the active batching CFJ and its consequences are limited to regulatory paperwork.
rows.sat_rat: Material classification is guided by established waste-management practice but still error-prone for mixed-material pharma equipment. Sorting decommissioned materials into disposal classes manually slows the whole teardown. Distinguishing contaminated from clean, recyclable from hazardous material requires trained judgement that not every crew has. Wrong classification means paying hazardous rates for benign material or costly recalls of misrouted waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection stoppage caused by hazardous stored energy in retired system components
rows.imp_rat: Inspection stoppage from stored energy in retired components (item 10) concerns decommissioned equipment, so relevance to active batching is minimal though the safety consequence is non-trivial. Well below ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Discharge procedures usually restore inspection quickly, but unexpected stored energy still causes intermittent unplanned stoppages. Stored energy in retired components forces inspection stoppages while it is bled down or made safe, directly costing schedule time. Rapidly and safely discharging stored energy to resume inspection requires trained technicians. The main loss is idle inspection time rather than materials.

rows.stk: Environmental health and safety personnel, waste management contractors.
rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of retained chemical residue streams assigned an incorrect regulatory classification
rows.imp_rat: Misclassifying retained chemical residue streams is a regulatory/administrative error with only modest severity and almost no relevance to executing the liquid batching job itself.
rows.sat_rat: Classification relies on manual judgement against complex regulations, so incorrect assignments recur despite established waste-broker practice. Classifying retained residue streams involves slow manual review of SDS and regulatory tables that delays shipment.
