Costs left out of proposal pricing become surprise spend, directly defeating budget accuracy.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the number of missing ordered components incorrectly recorded as received during inspection
rows.plain: Make sure every part that was ordered is actually present before marking it as received. This prevents missing parts from being overlooked.
rows.imp_rat: Missing components misrecorded as received is a receiving-inspection error, peripheral to batching function; severity capped at 1 per batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Packing-list and ERP receiving checks catch most shortfalls, though hurried or partial inspections still misrecord missing components. Line-item receiving reconciliation is a routine step that adds little time. Checking packing lists against received items is a low-skill clerical task. Recording a missing item as received causes downstream cost, but reconciliation itself is cheap.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of inconsistency between delivered-system identifiers and approved order documentation
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment delivered has the same identifying information as the equipment approved in the order. This helps confirm that the correct equipment arrived.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency between delivered identifiers and order documentation is a procurement-record defect, peripheral to the batching job; capped at 1.0 by batch position.
rows.sat_rat: Standard document-control and tag-number practices keep identifier inconsistencies fairly low, with only sporadic mismatches. Cross-checking delivered identifiers against approved order documents is a fast confirmation step. The comparison is straightforward and requires little specialized skill. The check is inexpensive relative to the cost of accepting mislabeled equipment.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of inspection delay caused by inaccessible or unsafe inspection areas after delivery
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to inspect delivered equipment when the inspection areas are hard to reach or not safe to enter. This helps staff check the equipment safely and sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Inspection delay from inaccessible areas after delivery is a site-access error remote from the core batching function; consequences are delayed acceptance. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Receiving-area preparation is a fairly established practice, so severe access blocks are occasional rather than chronic. Inspection is delayed when delivered equipment sits in areas that must first be made safe or accessible, adding days to acceptance. Judging access and safety needs a competent inspector or HSE officer, a role generally available at receiving sites. Re-scheduling inspectors and idle equipment adds some cost but is not the primary loss.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of transit damage remaining undetected before delivery acceptance
rows.plain: Make sure any damage from shipping is found before the delivered equipment is formally accepted. This protects the recipient from accepting equipment that arrived damaged.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected transit damage before acceptance can cause later failures, yet the batch-4 ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Shock/tilt indicators and visual checks reliably catch gross damage, but concealed damage to internal components still escapes acceptance. Receiving inspection for transit damage is a standard step that fits within normal delivery-acceptance timing. Spotting external transit damage needs only moderate inspector skill, though hidden internal damage requires more expertise. Inspection labor is low-cost and far cheaper than accepting a damaged unit, though hidden damage claims can be expensive.

rows.stk: Receiving personnel, project managers, commissioning engineers.
rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination remaining undetected on delivered system surfaces during receiving inspection
rows.plain: Make sure unwanted material left on the delivered equipment is noticed during the incoming inspection. This helps prevent dirty equipment from being accepted and used.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected contamination on delivered surfaces matters for product safety, but per the mandatory ceiling this batch cannot exceed 1.0; capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Visual and wipe checks catch obvious fouling, yet subtle internal or microbiological contamination often goes undetected until commissioning. Surface contamination checks add inspection time at receiving but are usually scheduled into the acceptance window. Detecting residual contamination reliably requires trained QC inspectors and sometimes swab/analytical methods not always on hand at receiving. Visual inspection is cheap, but missed contamination triggers costly rework or requalification.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of introducing contamination into the sterile-product fluid pathway while making piping connections
rows.plain: Keep harmful or unwanted material out of the path that carries the sterile product while the pipes are joined. This helps keep the product safe from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination introduced into the sterile fluid pathway while making connections is genuinely serious, but the batch-4 ceiling limits both proxies to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Aseptic connectors and sanitization protocols exist, but maintaining sterility while making connections remains one of the least reliably controlled risks in this stage. Any suspected contamination of the sterile fluid path while connecting forces re-sterilization cycles that heavily delay startup. Aseptic connection technique demands high skill and rigorous discipline, and errors are easy to make manually. Compromised sterile pathways can scrap batches and force costly re-sterilization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging batching-system nozzles or piping seals while aligning and securing the piping
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the machine's pipe ends or seals while lining up and fastening the pipes. This helps prevent leaks and equipment problems.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging nozzles or piping seals during install is a defect concern, but the batch ceiling constrains both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Flange alignment procedures and torque specs help, but reliance on manual finesse means seal and nozzle damage during fit-up still occurs with some regularity. Careful alignment and controlled tightening slows piping work but avoids the far larger delay of replacing a damaged nozzle or seal. Avoiding nozzle overload and seal damage depends heavily on experienced fitters using proper alignment technique and torque control, making this strongly skill-bound. A damaged nozzle or crushed seal is costly to replace and can require refabrication, but incidents are individually contained.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of account setup stalled by incomplete or conflicting access approvals
rows.plain: Set up a person’s account quickly instead of letting missing or conflicting approvals hold it up. This allows the person to begin their authorized work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Account setup stalled by access-approval conflicts is an administrative onboarding delay largely disconnected from batching liquid (relevance minimal), with trivial consequences for the CFJ.
rows.sat_rat: Approval workflows exist but are often manual and email-driven, so conflicts and incompleteness recur and reliably cause delay. Account setup stalled by incomplete or conflicting approvals is a chronic bottleneck where users wait days for sign-offs to reconcile. Resolving conflicting approvals depends on coordinators chasing the right authorities rather than any specialized technical skill. Stalled setup keeps commissioning teams idle and extends schedule, a real but bounded cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of newly established accounts with credentials that fail at initial sign-in
rows.plain: Make sure newly created user accounts work the first time the users sign in. This lets people begin their work without access problems.
rows.imp_rat: Credentials failing at first sign-in is a minor account-provisioning reliability nuisance with negligible bearing on preparing batches (both proxies minimal).
rows.sat_rat: Provisioning-then-verify practices catch most bad credentials, so initial sign-in failures are common annoyances but rarely persistent problems. A credential that fails at first sign-in causes a short reset cycle rather than a major delay. Diagnosing and reissuing a failed credential is routine help-desk work needing little expertise. The cost of a failed initial login is trivial, limited to a brief support interaction.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of ingredient sources that cannot be uniquely identified from project records and physical connections
rows.plain: Make sure every ingredient source can be identified as one specific source using the project records and the actual connections.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguous ingredient-source identification is a documentation traceability gap, indirect to the running job (low relevance); ceiling sets severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Tag numbering, P&IDs and line-list documentation exist as standard practice, but gaps between physical connections and records still leave ambiguous sources fairly often. Tracing ambiguous ingredient sources back through records and connections is a slow, manual reconciliation task when tagging is incomplete. Correct identification depends on diligent labeling discipline and someone who knows the P&IDs, so it is vulnerable to human oversight. Mislabeled or unidentifiable sources risk wasted batches and rework, but the direct cost of tagging itself is low.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of contamination being introduced into sterile product-contact paths during integrity testing
rows.plain: Keep germs or other unwanted material from getting into the parts that touch the product while checking that the system is properly sealed. This helps protect the product from becoming unsafe.
rows.imp_rat: Contamination of sterile paths during integrity testing could in principle be serious, but the mandatory batch-4 ceiling forces relevance and severity to 1 since it ranks below all prior items.
rows.sat_rat: Closed single-use assemblies, sterile connectors and validated test protocols substantially reduce ingress risk, yet occasional breach events at manual connection points remain the recurring gap. Sterile integrity testing already follows defined SOPs that pace the work predictably, though aseptic technique adds setup and hold time that cannot be rushed. Preventing contamination during integrity testing depends heavily on trained aseptic operators and correct gowning/connection discipline, so outcomes still hinge on scarce human vigilance. Single-use sterile connectors, filters and clean consumables add recurring cost but are routinely budgeted as part of validated practice.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component rupture or uncontrolled pressure release during integrity testing
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment does not break or release pressure suddenly while it is being checked for leaks and strength. An uncontrolled release could injure people or damage the system.
rows.imp_rat: Component rupture during integrity testing is a safety event, yet the batch-4 ceiling forces both proxies to 1 as it ranks below all previously scored items.
rows.sat_rat: Pressure relief devices, rated components and automated ramp control make uncontrolled rupture during testing an uncommon event where standard practice is followed. Pressure-hold and diffusion tests are quick, standardized procedures that rarely extend the preparation timeline. Automated integrity testers with preset pressure limits reduce reliance on operator judgement, though rig-up still needs a competent technician. Relief valves, rated tubing and calibrated testers are modest, well-established investments relative to the rupture hazard they prevent.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the installation foundation cannot safely support the batching system loads
rows.plain: Make sure the floor or base under the mixing system is strong and safe enough to carry its weight and operating loads. This helps prevent movement, damage, or collapse.
rows.imp_rat: An unsafe foundation for system loads is a stability concern, but the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1 for this batch.
rows.sat_rat: Engineered foundation design with documented load data is a well-established practice, making an inadequate foundation an uncommon failure when the design basis is correct. Foundation adequacy is established during design and site prep well before installation, so it rarely delays the preparation stage itself. Structural and civil engineers size foundations against known loads using mature methods, reducing dependence on field judgement. Foundations and load verification are a planned capital cost, and over-design margins are standard rather than a surprise expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tanks, pumps, and metering hardware positioned outside specified locations or orientations
rows.plain: Place tanks, pumps, and measuring equipment in the required positions and directions. This helps the equipment work correctly and safely.
rows.imp_rat: Tanks, pumps, and metering hardware placed outside specified locations is an install-layout error tangential to running a batch (relevance) with low direct harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensioned drawings and installation checks make correct positioning the norm, with only occasional orientation errors remaining. Repositioning mislocated tanks, pumps, or metering hardware adds rework time but is usually caught early. Following layout drawings to place equipment correctly is routine work for competent installers. Relocating heavy hardware after the fact carries rigging and labor cost when errors go uncaught.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of component mounting interfaces being misaligned with prepared foundation anchors
rows.plain: Make sure the equipment’s attachment points line up with the anchors already set in the foundation. Misalignment could make installation difficult or leave the equipment unstable.
rows.imp_rat: Misaligned mounting interfaces cause rework, yet the ranking places this item lower so both proxies remain at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Anchor-bolt templates and as-built surveys reduce misalignment, but concrete pour tolerances and template errors still routinely produce mounting-interface mismatches. Anchor misalignment is typically caught at set-down and forces time-consuming re-drilling or shimming on the critical path. Accurate anchor placement and correction relies on skilled millwrights and surveyors working from templates. Rework of misplaced anchors means chipping concrete, epoxy anchors and lost labor, a moderate but common expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reference requests unanswered before the supplier selection decision
rows.plain: Get answers to all requested user references before choosing a supplier. This gives the buyer useful feedback from people who have already used the supplier.
rows.imp_rat: Unanswered reference requests before selection is a due-diligence gap peripheral to the batching job (low relevance); the mandatory ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Reference outreach is ad hoc and low-response, so a meaningful share stay unanswered when the selection is made. Reference responses often lag or never arrive before the decision deadline, forcing choices on incomplete input. Getting references answered is more about persistence and relationships than specialized skill. Unanswered references carry little direct cost, only decision risk.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of reference users whose operating settings are not comparable to the intended sterile fill-finish environment
rows.plain: Make sure reference users work in conditions similar to the planned sterile drug-filling operation. Their experience is more useful when their equipment and work setting are comparable.
rows.imp_rat: Reference users whose operating settings differ from the sterile fill-finish environment is a benchmarking-comparability issue with low relevance to batching the liquid itself, and its consequences are indirect; capped at 1.0 by batch-4 ranking.
rows.sat_rat: References are routinely offered from dissimilar (non-sterile or lab-scale) installations, so buyers frequently act on non-comparable evidence with no standard way to verify comparability. Chasing down reference-site operating conditions comparable to a sterile fill-finish context adds weeks of site visits and questionnaires during acquisition. Judging whether a reference user's environment truly matches sterile fill-finish demands scarce GMP process expertise that few buying teams possess. The status quo relies on travel and consultant time to vet references, but the direct spend is modest relative to the risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree to which reference accounts are biased by supplier screening or participation
rows.plain: Make sure other customers’ opinions are not shaped only by which customers the supplier chose or allowed to take part.
rows.imp_rat: Reference-account bias from supplier screening is a peripheral procurement-vetting concern with low relevance to actually batching and preparing liquids, and consequences are mild; capped at 1.0 by batch ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers almost always hand-pick favorable references, and buyers have no established mechanism to counter this selection bias. Screening for reference bias is rarely done, so it adds no time but also yields no assurance. Recognizing that supplier-curated references are skewed requires evaluator sophistication that is inconsistently present. Biased references impose no direct cost but raise the risk of a costly wrong pick.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of decision-critical operating detail withheld because of confidentiality restrictions
rows.plain: Get as much important information about how the equipment operates as possible, even when people cannot share some details because they are confidential.
rows.imp_rat: Operating detail withheld due to confidentiality is an information-transfer friction with low centrality to batching and modest impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers routinely withhold operating detail citing confidentiality, and buyers lack reliable leverage to obtain it pre-award. NDA negotiation to unlock withheld detail slows the information-gathering phase. Extracting decision-critical detail past confidentiality barriers requires skilled negotiation and technical questioning. Confidentiality limits impose no direct cost but risk under-informed, costlier decisions.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of evaluation criteria with conflicting peer accounts of system performance in sterile fill-finish use
rows.plain: Make sure people giving references agree about how well the system performs when used to fill and seal sterile products. Conflicting accounts make it harder to judge whether the system is suitable.
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting peer accounts on sterile fill-finish performance is an evaluation-ambiguity error in sourcing, not central to batching itself (low relevance); ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Peer accounts of performance in this specialized use frequently conflict, and there is no standard method to reconcile them. Reconciling conflicting peer accounts requires extra follow-up interviews that stretch evaluation. Judging which conflicting reference account to trust for sterile fill-finish performance needs deep domain familiarity. The cost is mainly evaluation effort and selection risk rather than direct spend.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the supplier's committed completion date falls after the required site-delivery date
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier can finish the order early enough for it to arrive at the work site by the required date. This helps keep the work on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: A supplier completion date slipping past required site delivery is a scheduling error tangential to liquid batching function (low relevance); mandatory batch-5 ceiling keeps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Delivery-date commitments in this niche are notoriously variable because engineered batching systems have long, uncertain fabrication cycles that standard PO terms poorly enforce. Suppliers routinely quote optimistic lead times and the status quo relies on manual schedule tracking, so committed dates slipping past required delivery is a chronic, late-discovered problem. Experienced procurement staff can probe for realistic delivery commitments, but this judgement is scarce and inconsistently applied across buyers. Late commitments trigger expedite fees, penalties, or idle downstream resources, but the direct monetary hit is secondary to the schedule slip itself.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that critical custom components are unavailable when manufacturing must begin
rows.plain: Make sure important specially made parts will be available when manufacturing needs to start.
rows.imp_rat: Unavailable custom components at manufacturing start is a supply-chain risk upstream of operation; despite real stakes, the batch ordering caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Custom long-lead items frequently slip, and status quo scheduling and expediting only partly protect the manufacturing start date. Long lead times on critical custom components mean availability gaps at manufacturing start are common and hard to compress. Managing custom-component procurement schedules requires skilled supply-chain planners who are often overloaded. Expediting or holding buffer stock for custom items is costly, and shortfalls halt production expensively.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of specified configuration requirements omitted from the supplier's commitment
rows.plain: Make sure the supplier agrees to every required setup or feature. This prevents important requirements from being left out of the supplier's promise.
rows.imp_rat: Configuration requirements omitted from a supplier commitment is a contractual-completeness error, indirect to the CFJ (low relevance); cap sets severity to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Suppliers omit or reinterpret specified requirements in their commitments, and matrix-based traceability is inconsistently applied. Cross-checking each specified requirement against the supplier's written commitment is a slow line-by-line exercise. Detecting silently dropped requirements in a supplier's response takes an experienced technical reviewer. Requirements the supplier never committed to become paid change orders later, a real financial exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for the supplier to provide a firm manufacturing and component-availability commitment
rows.plain: Get a definite promise from the supplier about when the item can be made and whether its parts are available. This helps the buyer know when the order can realistically be delivered.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for a firm manufacturing/availability commitment is a pre-purchase delay with limited relevance to preparing liquid batches and easily-absorbed consequences; held at the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Firm availability commitments are frequently provisional given component shortages, so certainty remains low. Waiting for a supplier's firm manufacturing and component-availability commitment can stretch out due to their own supply-chain checks. Extracting a firm commitment takes a buyer who knows how to press for confirmed lead times, a moderately specialized negotiation skill. The delay defers the schedule more than it wastes money directly.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that confirmed component availability changes before purchase terms are finalized
rows.plain: Make sure the parts that are currently available will still be available when the purchase agreement is completed. A change could delay the order.
rows.imp_rat: The error of component availability changing before purchase terms finalize is a procurement-timing concern peripheral to actually batching liquids (low relevance); per the batch-5 ceiling and its ranking below prior items, both proxies are capped at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Supplier quotes and lead-time confirmations are only weakly binding, so confirmed availability frequently shifts before finalization. Confirmed availability can change during the window before purchase terms close, and re-checking lags real supplier status. Keeping availability confirmations current demands diligent buyer follow-up that is easy to let lapse. A reversed availability commitment forces costly resourcing or expediting after terms were set.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of personnel injury or batching-system damage during initial energization
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that people get hurt or the mixing equipment is damaged when power and other services are turned on for the first time. This helps make the startup safe.
rows.imp_rat: Personnel injury during energization is inherently high-stakes, but the batch-4 ceiling explicitly caps both proxies at 1 for all items here.
rows.sat_rat: LOTO, pre-energization inspections and staged power-up are mature practices that make injury or equipment damage at first power infrequent, though wiring or setpoint errors still occasionally slip through. Energization is gated by pre-power checklists and lockout/tagout steps that add controlled time but keep the sequence orderly. Safe first energization leans on qualified electricians and commissioning engineers whose judgement remains essential to catch wiring errors. The cost is mainly labor for pre-checks plus occasional damaged components, which is contained by disciplined commissioning.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of deviation of connected utility supplies from specified voltage, pressure, flow, and quality requirements
rows.plain: Make sure the incoming electricity and other services meet the required levels for power, pressure, amount, and cleanliness. This helps the machine run safely and reliably.
rows.imp_rat: Utility supply deviations affect operation, yet the batch ceiling requires both proxies to be held at 1 for this lower-ranked set.
rows.sat_rat: Utility acceptance checks and site-readiness protocols catch most deviations before startup, though marginal or fluctuating plant supplies remain a recurring source of trouble. Utility verification is a defined pre-start step, but chasing out-of-spec supplies can cause real delays when plant utilities fall short. Interpreting voltage, pressure, flow and quality readings against spec requires competent commissioning staff but is well-documented routine work. Metering, regulators and filtration to correct supplies add cost, and undersized utilities can force expensive remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of hook-up delay caused by unavailable required utility sources or inaccessible connection points
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to connect power and other needed services when the sources or connection points cannot be reached. This helps the equipment be set up sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Hook-up delays from unavailable utilities or inaccessible connections are setup logistics peripheral to batching (relevance) with low harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Utility readiness depends on upstream coordination that is inconsistently managed, so gaps surface repeatedly at hook-up. Hook-up frequently stalls when required utilities or accessible connection points are not ready, a recurring source of startup delay. Identifying and routing utilities takes moderate craft skill but is within normal maintenance capability. Waiting crews and idle equipment during utility unavailability impose labor and schedule costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of operator qualification delays caused by unavailable prerequisites, trainers, or batching system access
rows.plain: Avoid delays in preparing workers to run the system when required materials, instructors, or system access are missing. This lets operators become ready to work sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Operator qualification delays from missing prerequisites are a scheduling issue tangential to executing a batch (relevance) with low direct harm (severity); capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Scheduling and resource-tracking practices are ad hoc, so prerequisite and access conflicts recur unpredictably despite planning. Qualification delays from unavailable trainers, prerequisites, or blocked system access are a chronic scheduling pain that routinely stretches operator readiness timelines. Availability of qualified trainers is itself a scarce-skill bottleneck, and no standard practice reliably decouples qualification from that dependency. Idle operators and stalled equipment during these delays impose real labor and opportunity costs on the preparation stage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of structural supports or modules shifting or collapsing during mechanical assembly
rows.plain: Avoid allowing supports or parts of the system to move or fall during assembly. This helps prevent injuries and damage to the system.
rows.imp_rat: Structural supports shifting during assembly is a safety concern, but the batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Lift plans, temporary supports and sequenced assembly are standard rigging practice that make shifting or collapse during assembly uncommon, though setup errors occasionally cause instability. Temporary bracing and staged lifting add setup time but keep assembly moving safely without major stoppages. Safe assembly relies on competent riggers and lift planning, keeping the task dependent on experienced crews. Cribbing, bracing and rigging gear are modest recurring costs against the severe expense of a collapse.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of particulate or microbial contamination introduced into product-contact areas during assembly
rows.plain: Keep dust and germs from getting onto surfaces that will touch the product while the parts are being put together. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Particulate or microbial contamination in product-contact areas during assembly is genuinely consequential, but the mandatory batch-4 ceiling limits both proxies to 1.0 regardless.
rows.sat_rat: Controlled-environment assembly and flushing reduce contamination, but introduction during hands-on assembly remains a persistent, hard-to-eliminate risk. Particulate or microbial contamination found later triggers re-cleaning and re-testing that lengthens the stage. Preventing ingress during assembly requires trained cleanroom discipline and constant vigilance that is hard to sustain. Contamination remediation wastes materials and can scrap affected components.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterile-contact surfaces or seals damaged during handling and assembly
rows.plain: Avoid damaging surfaces or seals that must stay free of germs while putting the system parts together. This helps prevent contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Damaged sterile-contact surfaces or seals during handling is a quality-affecting defect, but batch-4 positioning caps both proxies at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handling procedures and protective packaging help, but seal and surface damage during assembly still recurs with manual handling. Replacing seals or surfaces damaged in handling adds procurement and rework time. Avoiding damage to sterile-contact surfaces and seals depends heavily on careful, skilled handling technique. Damaged sterile components are often scrapped and replaced, an outright material loss.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of supplied modules with mechanical interfaces that do not mate to adjoining modules as specified
rows.plain: Avoid receiving system parts whose connecting pieces do not fit together as planned. This helps the system go together and work properly.
rows.imp_rat: Non-mating mechanical module interfaces cause assembly problems, but the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping relevance and severity.
rows.sat_rat: Dimensional drawings and factory acceptance testing reduce mismatch risk, but tolerance stack-up and vendor coordination gaps still produce interfaces that do not mate as specified. Interface mismatches discovered at assembly cause significant on-site delay while parts are reworked or refabricated. Skilled fitters can adapt many minor mismatches in the field, so the burden falls on craft labor when interfaces do not mate cleanly. Non-mating modules drive costly field rework, custom spools and shipping of replacements.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of foreign material entering product-contact piping during installation
rows.plain: Keep dust, debris, and other unwanted material out of the product-carrying pipes while they are being installed. This helps protect the product from contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Foreign material entering product-contact piping during installation is a real contamination risk, but the mandatory batch-4 ceiling limits both proxies to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: End caps, flushing, and inspection reduce ingress, but foreign material still enters product-contact piping during installation with some regularity. Foreign material discovered in piping forces disassembly, flushing, and re-inspection that delay startup. Preventing debris ingress relies on disciplined capping and housekeeping practice that demands sustained attention. Foreign-material removal and re-flushing waste cleaning resources and time.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in the documented access permission structures
rows.plain: Clearly record who is allowed to access the system and what each person is allowed to do. This helps prevent unauthorized changes or use.
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in documented access permission structures is an administrative governance concern with minimal centrality to batching liquid (relevance minimal) and low direct consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Access permission documents are commonly informal or inconsistent at preparation time, so ambiguity is a routine source of misconfiguration rather than an occasional one. Ambiguous access documentation slows every downstream provisioning decision as people stop to interpret intent. Interpreting a vague permission structure demands experienced staff who can infer what was meant, offloading effort onto scarce judgement. Ambiguity causes rework and misconfiguration whose costs accumulate quietly over the project.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of the batch hold while awaiting quality verification results
rows.plain: Spend less time keeping a medicine batch on hold while waiting for quality test results. This helps the batch be released or addressed sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Batch hold awaiting quality verification is an inherent gating delay (relevance) with minimal downside since it protects quality (severity); held at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Off-line lab testing is reliable in accuracy but the turnaround is inconsistent and rarely fast enough to avoid the hold. Waiting for lab QC results routinely holds batches for tens of minutes to hours, a chronic bottleneck between preparation and release. The delay stems from lab analytical work requiring skilled chemists, and inline alternatives demanding specialized validation. Held batches tie up capital and vessel capacity, and lab testing carries direct labor and reagent cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of operator exposure to hazardous ingredient material during loading
rows.plain: Protect workers from breathing in or touching dangerous ingredient material while loading it. This helps prevent harm to their health.
rows.imp_rat: Operator exposure to hazardous ingredient during loading is a safety concern with serious potential consequences, yet the batch-4 ranking caps importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Closed-loop charging, dust containment and PPE protocols substantially reduce exposure, though manual charging of hazardous ingredients still exposes operators when engineering controls are absent. Safe loading procedures add some handling time but this is not the primary concern. Proper use of PPE and containment requires training and discipline that varies across operators. Closed transfer systems and containment equipment carry capital cost but are increasingly standard.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of waiting for the batching system to accept the start command after all start conditions are satisfied
rows.plain: Have the mixing system begin its work quickly once every required condition is ready. This avoids wasting time after the operator has given the start instruction.
rows.imp_rat: Waiting for the system to accept a start command after conditions are met is a minor latency nuisance—low relevance to the CFJ and trivial severity. Capped at 2.0.
rows.sat_rat: Acceptance is consistent and predictable, though older or heavily loaded control networks occasionally add erratic delay. Once all conditions are met, modern controllers accept and act on the start command within seconds, though scan cycles and handshake delays introduce a noticeable lag on some legacy systems. No operator skill affects this acceptance latency; it is a function of the control architecture. The short acceptance delay wastes little more than a few seconds of operator attention per batch.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batching system unavailability caused by between-batch rinsing
rows.plain: Keep the equipment that prepares products in batches from being unavailable for long while it is rinsed between batches. This helps the next batch start sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Between-batch rinsing downtime is a throughput-relevant delay with mild consequences, and the batch-4 ranking caps its importance at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: CIP cycle times are predictable and consistent, but the total downtime remains substantial and hard to compress without risking cleanliness. Between-batch rinsing is a significant, recurring source of system unavailability that directly eats into productive time. Rinsing is largely automated so it demands little skill, but sequencing it around production requires planning. The unavailability represents lost production capacity plus rinse consumables.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of rinse-fluid leaks or spills during between-batch rinsing
rows.plain: Avoid spilling or leaking rinse liquid while cleaning between production runs. This helps keep people, equipment, and the work area safe and clean.
rows.imp_rat: Rinse-fluid leaks or spills during rinsing is a waste/housekeeping error of low centrality and minor consequence; ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Closed CIP loops, gasketed connections and drip trays make spills fairly uncommon, with leaks mainly from aging seals or misaligned hoses. Cleaning up an occasional rinse spill adds minor unplanned time. Preventing leaks is mostly about equipment integrity, not operator skill. Leaks waste rinse fluid and chemicals and create effluent handling cost, though volumes are usually small.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of required batch, audit, or service data unintentionally erased while removing proprietary data
rows.plain: Avoid accidentally deleting important production, inspection, or service records while removing the company's private information. This matters because the needed records must still be available afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Erasing required batch or audit data during proprietary-data removal risks compliance loss, but it is a decommissioning-data error ranked below prior items (low relevance); ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Sanitizing proprietary data without destroying retained records is poorly tooled, so unintended erasure is a real and inadequately mitigated risk. Careful selective deletion is slower than bulk wiping, creating pressure that raises accidental-erasure risk. Separating proprietary data from records that must be retained requires data-classification expertise rarely present at disposal. Unintentionally erased batch or audit data can be irrecoverable, forcing costly reconstruction or compliance exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of transfer or scrapping delays caused by unresolved deletion and retention requirements
rows.plain: Shorten the delay in transferring or scrapping equipment while people are still deciding what information must be deleted and what must be kept. This helps the equipment move on sooner while handling information properly.
rows.imp_rat: Transfer or scrapping delays from unresolved retention rules are end-of-life timing errors remote from batching (low relevance); ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Retention and deletion requirements are frequently unclear at disposal, so delays from unresolved questions are predictable and recurring. Unresolved deletion and retention questions stall transfer or scrapping until legal and compliance sign off, a common bottleneck. Resolving retention-versus-deletion conflicts needs records-management and legal judgement that disposal teams lack. Delays incur storage and standby costs while assets await disposition clearance.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removed proprietary data being repopulated on the controller before transfer, resale, or scrapping
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that deleted company information is restored to the machine before it is transferred, sold, or discarded. This helps keep private information from being exposed.
rows.imp_rat: Repopulation of proprietary data on the controller before transfer or scrapping is a data-hygiene/end-of-life concern peripheral to preparing liquid batches, with contained consequences; ceiling of 1.0 applies.
rows.sat_rat: Without chain-of-custody controls, wiped controllers can be re-imaged from backups, and this risk is rarely reliably managed. Confirming data is not restored before transfer adds a verification step often bypassed. Preventing repopulation from backups requires understanding of the system's data architecture that few disposal crews possess. Repopulated data undermining a resale or scrap plan can incur IP and remediation costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of recovered components that retain unacceptable process residues
rows.plain: Make sure recovered parts do not still have unacceptable leftover material or chemicals on them. Clean parts are safer and more suitable for reuse.
rows.imp_rat: Recovered components retaining residues is a decommissioning/recycling concern removed from active batching (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Cleaning at disposal is not held to production-grade verification, so recovered components frequently retain unacceptable residues. Achieving and verifying clean recovered components extends teardown time. Removing residues from complex product-contact geometry requires skilled cleaning technicians. Components retaining residue are downgraded or scrapped, wasting recovery value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of salvageable pumps, valves, sensors, and vessels damaged during extraction
rows.plain: Avoid damaging pumps, valves, sensors, and tanks that could still be used while removing them. This preserves useful equipment and reduces replacement costs.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging salvageable pumps/valves during extraction is an asset-recovery error peripheral to the batching function with limited severity; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without standardized salvage-extraction procedures, damage rates vary greatly and are commonly underestimated. Gentle extraction of pumps, valves, and sensors is slower than demolition-style removal. Preserving salvageable precision components demands skilled technicians familiar with each device's fragility. Damage during extraction directly reduces resale or reuse recovery value, a real financial loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a recovered component has undetected degradation
rows.plain: Make sure a part recovered for reuse does not have hidden wear or damage. This helps prevent an unsafe or unreliable part from being used again.
rows.imp_rat: Undetected degradation in a recovered component (relevance 1) sits below the prior batch and is capped; while a hidden fault could cause a later batch failure (severity 1), the ranking places it at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is little standard practice to systematically detect hidden degradation on retired components, so this remains a chronic blind spot. Inspecting recovered components for degradation is time-consuming and often skipped under disposal timelines. Detecting undetected degradation requires metallurgical or NDT expertise rarely applied at disposal. Undetected degradation mostly matters if components are reused, but assessment cost is often deemed not worthwhile at end of life.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of recovered components with missing identity or service-history records
rows.plain: Fewer recovered parts should be missing records showing what they are or how they were used and maintained. This information helps determine whether they can be safely reused.
rows.imp_rat: Missing identity/service-history on recovered components matters to asset recovery admin but is peripheral to preparing and batching liquid; consequences are traceability gaps, not batch failure. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Records are routinely lost between CMMS, paper files, and removed tags, so missing-identity recovery is chronic and largely unsolved. Locating and attaching service history to each recovered component is slow manual archaeology. Reconstructing missing identity and maintenance records needs experienced staff who recall the equipment. Components lacking provenance fetch far less on resale and may be scrapped, a clear cost loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that recyclable materials are rejected because of residual pharmaceutical or cleaning substances
rows.plain: Make sure recyclable materials are free of leftover medicines or cleaning products before sending them for recycling. This prevents the materials from being rejected or creating a safety problem.
rows.imp_rat: Rejection of recyclables for residues is an end-of-life waste concern outside the core batching job (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Pre-recycling decontamination is inconsistent for pharma equipment, so residual substances still cause recycler rejections fairly often. Rejected recyclables must be re-cleaned and re-submitted, adding turnaround time. Achieving recycler-acceptable residue levels requires cleaning know-how applied to end-of-life parts. Rejected recyclables revert to costly hazardous disposal, raising expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of system materials assigned to incompatible recycling streams
rows.plain: Send each system material to the correct recycling category rather than putting it in the wrong one. This helps the materials get recycled properly and avoids contamination.
rows.imp_rat: Materials assigned to incompatible recycling streams is a disposal-classification error (relevance 1) with compliance-adjacent but low functional stakes (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without reliable composition data, mixed and composite parts are frequently assigned to wrong streams, leaving recurring gaps. Sorting materials to correct streams adds sorting time that is sometimes shortcut. Correctly identifying and separating mixed-material assemblies requires material knowledge that on-site crews often lack. Misrouted materials lose recycling value and can incur contamination penalties.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of components whose material composition cannot be verified before routing
rows.plain: Make sure the material each component is made from can be confirmed before it is sent for recycling. This helps ensure materials are handled and recycled correctly.
rows.imp_rat: Unverifiable material composition before routing is a disposal/routing concern (relevance 1) with limited batching consequences (severity 1); held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Original material certifications are often lost by disposal, so many components cannot be verified and get routed on assumption. Verifying material composition before routing is slow when documentation is missing and testing is needed. Alloy or polymer identification requires XRF tools or lab analysis and trained interpretation not typically on hand. Composition testing or conservative disposal to avoid uncertainty adds cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that applicable recycling channels refuse separated system materials
rows.plain: Make sure the separated materials from the system are accepted by the appropriate recycling services. This prevents recyclable materials from being refused or sent to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Recycling channels refusing separated materials is an end-of-life disposal issue (relevance 1) far from active batching, with low operational stakes (severity 1); held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycling channels for common metals and plastics accept most materials, though contamination or specialty alloys occasionally trigger refusal. Confirming recycler acceptance can require lead time to arrange, occasionally delaying material routing. Waste brokers and recyclers guide acceptance, reducing the skill burden on the facility. Rejected materials incur return, re-handling, or landfill fees, a moderate recurring cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration separated recyclable materials remain onsite awaiting transfer
rows.plain: Move separated recyclable materials offsite sooner. This keeps storage areas from becoming crowded while the materials wait for pickup.
rows.imp_rat: Recyclable materials awaiting transfer onsite is a waste-handling delay error remote from liquid batching; consequences are storage congestion. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Established recycling and scrap-hauler arrangements make transfer fairly consistent with only minor delays. Recyclable materials sometimes linger onsite awaiting haulers but pickup is generally arranged within normal cycles. Managing recyclable transfer is routine waste-handling work requiring little expertise. Onsite storage of recyclables carries low cost and materials retain scrap value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of legal ownership of the batching system remaining disputed after transfer
rows.plain: Make clear who legally owns the mixing system after it is handed over. This prevents later arguments about ownership and responsibility.
rows.imp_rat: Disputed legal ownership after transfer is a contractual/title error peripheral to the batching system's function; consequences are legal exposure but not operational failure. Capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Bills of sale, asset transfer agreements, and title documentation make clean ownership transfer the norm, with disputes rare. Legal ownership transfer paperwork can lag but is typically completed around the transfer event. Clearing title requires legal and asset-management input that is generally available for major disposals. Disputed ownership can expose the firm to liability or lost salvage value, though this is uncommon.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of required validation, maintenance, and safety records that are missing or inconsistent at handover
rows.plain: Make sure the new owner receives complete and consistent records about testing, upkeep, and safety. This matters so they can operate and care for the system safely after the handover.
rows.imp_rat: Missing validation/maintenance/safety records at handover is administrative documentation, peripheral to producing batches; severity kept at 1 per the mandatory batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Handover record sets are commonly incomplete or inconsistent because record-keeping over a system's life is fragmented across systems and people, with only partial checklists to catch gaps. Chasing down scattered validation, maintenance, and safety records at handover consistently delays closeout because they were never consolidated in advance. Knowing which records are required and where they live relies on institutional memory that is often gone by decommissioning time. Reconstructing missing records or re-doing lost documentation adds avoidable labor and rework cost at handover.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batching system components damaged during removal and relocation
rows.plain: Avoid damaging parts of the material-mixing system when it is taken apart and moved. This helps keep the equipment usable and reduces repair or replacement work.
rows.imp_rat: Components damaged during removal and relocation is a handling error tangential to the batching CFJ itself, with recoverable impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Rigging plans and lifting procedures reduce damage, but shared-line congestion and rushed schedules still cause frequent incidents. Careful disassembly and rigging for relocation extends teardown timelines. Safe removal and handling of batching components relies on experienced riggers and millwrights. Damaged components during relocation directly erode salvage or reuse value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of disruption to adjacent active equipment caused by disconnecting a shared interface
rows.plain: Disconnect a shared connection without interrupting nearby equipment that is still operating. This lets the work continue while other equipment remains in use.
rows.imp_rat: Disruption to adjacent equipment from disconnecting a shared interface is a decommissioning-phase error peripheral to the batching CFJ; consequences are localized downtime. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Isolation planning and blinding procedures exist but shared-interface surprises still cause unplanned adjacent-equipment downtime. Disconnecting shared interfaces often forces extended shutdowns of adjacent equipment that are hard to minimize. Isolating shared utilities without disrupting live equipment requires skilled process and safety engineers. Disruption to active adjacent equipment can halt production, imposing significant cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging facility-side piping or fittings while separating connections
rows.plain: Avoid damaging the site's pipes or connecting parts while taking the connections apart. This helps keep the site's piping usable and leak-free.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging facility piping during disconnection is a defect risk, but the mandatory ceiling holds relevance and severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard lockout, isolation, and mechanical disconnection practices make facility-side damage uncommon when procedures are followed. Careful disconnection to protect facility piping adds modest time but is a routine planned activity. Skilled millwrights and pipefitters generally know how to isolate and separate connections without damaging facility-side fittings. Occasional damage to facility piping incurs repair cost but is usually contained.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of process parameters transferred to the replacement system that differ from approved settings
rows.plain: Make sure the operating settings moved to the replacement system are the same as the approved settings, so the process works as intended.
rows.imp_rat: Transferred parameters differing from approved settings could affect quality, but the batch-4 ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Change-control and validation practice during system replacement reliably reconciles most parameters, though manual re-entry still introduces occasional deviations. Transferring approved parameters to a replacement system is a defined data-migration task with predictable duration. Verifying transferred settings against approved values requires trained validation engineers. Parameter deviations can spoil batches on the new system, an avoidable cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of production batches without a clear system assignment during the transition
rows.plain: Fewer production batches should be left without a clear connection to the system that made them during the changeover. This makes each batch easier to identify and track.
rows.imp_rat: Batches without clear system assignment during transition touches production but as a transitional admin gap; the ranking places it below higher batches, so relevance and severity stay at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Transition planning tools exist but batch-to-system mapping gaps still occur during cutover, leaving some batches unassigned. Reassigning production batches during transition can stall if system ownership is unclear. Correct batch reassignment requires production planners who understand both old and new system capabilities. Misassigned batches risk scrapped or delayed product, a direct production cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of operational responsibilities without clear ownership after handover
rows.plain: Make sure every ongoing work duty has a clearly named owner after the handover. This prevents important duties from being left undone.
rows.imp_rat: Operational responsibilities lacking ownership after handover is a governance/handover error with only indirect bearing on batching function; consequences are coordination confusion. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Handover often relies on informal RACI or meeting notes, leaving responsibilities ambiguous and frequently orphaned. Clarifying ownership of residual responsibilities during handover often drags on past the transition. Defining who owns each remaining operational duty needs managerial judgement across functions. Ownership gaps create low direct cost but risk costly lapses in safety or compliance duties.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of major components or subsystems omitted from the final condition record
rows.plain: Record the condition of every major part of the system at the end, so problems are not overlooked and others know what they are receiving.
rows.imp_rat: Components omitted from the final condition record is an end-of-life documentation gap, low relevance to producing batches; forced to 1.0 by the ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Final condition records are typically checklist-driven yet still omit minor or hidden subsystems because coverage is not systematically enforced. Walking down every component for the final condition record takes significant time that decommissioning schedules often compress. Knowing which subsystems and components must be captured requires competent asset knowledge, not just a walkthrough. Thorough condition recording costs inspection labor but avoids downstream disputes over omitted assets.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that operational condition cannot be verified after supporting utilities are disconnected
rows.plain: Make it unlikely that the machine’s working condition cannot be checked after its supporting equipment and connections are turned off or removed. This helps create a reliable record of its final condition.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to verify operational condition after utilities are disconnected relates to decommissioning (relevance 1) and its consequences are contained (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Standard practice does not capture verified operational state before de-energizing, so condition frequently cannot be confirmed afterward. Once utilities are disconnected there is a narrow window and verifying operational condition afterward is often impractical, causing delays if attempted. Confirming operational condition post-disconnection needs test-rig setups and technicians familiar with the system. Re-energizing or rigging temporary utilities to verify condition is costly and seldom justified at disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition entries associated with the wrong component or subsystem identifier
rows.plain: Make sure each condition note is linked to the correct part or section of the system. This prevents people from making decisions based on information about the wrong item.
rows.imp_rat: Condition entries tied to the wrong identifier is a data-mapping error with low centrality to preparing liquids and minor consequence; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Manual, spreadsheet-based condition logging with degraded or removed tags makes wrong-component entries a common, poorly caught error. Correctly matching condition entries to component IDs slows the assessment step when tags are missing. Accurate association demands personnel who know the system's tagging and P&ID conventions. Misassigned condition data causes downstream valuation errors and rework costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sterility-critical interlocks, alarms, or sequence steps that behave incorrectly after transfer
rows.plain: Avoid having important safety connections, warnings, or operating steps work incorrectly after the control settings are moved to another system. This helps the equipment keep operating safely.
rows.imp_rat: Sterility-critical interlocks behaving incorrectly after transfer is a contamination risk, yet the batch ceiling requires both proxies remain at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Transferred sterility interlocks are frequently mis-mapped and current requalification testing catches only a fraction, leaving this poorly served. Verifying interlock and alarm behavior after transfer adds substantial testing time that is often compressed under restart pressure. Validating sterility-critical sequence logic requires specialized validation and controls expertise that few staff possess. An interlock behaving incorrectly can scrap sterile batches, making the cost of failure very high.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of required control logic, parameters, and automation settings absent from the replacement configuration at cutover
rows.plain: Put all needed operating instructions, settings, and automatic controls into the replacement system before it is put into use, so it behaves as expected.
rows.imp_rat: Missing control logic and automation settings at cutover is relevant to correct batching operation, but the batch ceiling caps this item; severity of an incomplete replacement configuration is constrained here.
rows.sat_rat: Status quo relies on manual configuration inventories and spreadsheets that routinely omit undocumented tweaks, so undetected gaps at cutover remain common. Configuration transfer at cutover is a slow, manual reconciliation that stretches decommissioning timelines when logic is discovered missing late. Correctly capturing every control parameter and automation setting demands scarce automation engineers who understand both the retiring and replacement systems. Missed logic surfaces as rework and post-cutover firefighting, driving up commissioning cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of selecting a superseded or unapproved retiring-system configuration as the migration source
rows.plain: Avoid choosing an outdated or unapproved setup from the system being replaced as the starting point for the move. Using the wrong setup could cause errors in the new system.
rows.imp_rat: Selecting a superseded configuration as migration source is a data-migration risk at retirement, peripheral to active batching; ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Weak or ambiguous version control on retiring systems makes selecting a superseded configuration a real and recurring risk with few safeguards. Verifying which configuration is the approved current one adds time when revision histories are unclear. Distinguishing an approved baseline from a superseded one demands configuration-management expertise that is often thin at disposal. Migrating from the wrong source configuration causes costly rework or invalid replacement builds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of transferred configuration elements lacking traceability to an approved source record required for release
rows.plain: Make sure every moved setting can be traced back to an approved record before the system is put into use. This shows where each setting came from and supports safe approval.
rows.imp_rat: Configuration elements lacking traceability to approved source records affect release integrity, but ranking places it below prior items; capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Legacy systems often lack complete approved source records, so a meaningful share of configuration elements cannot be traced for release. Tracing each transferred configuration element to an approved source record is a slow, document-intensive effort. Establishing traceability for release requires quality and validation specialists who understand data integrity requirements. Gaps in traceability trigger costly investigations and delay release.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile fill-finish production restart delay caused by configuration migration at cutover
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to restart germ-free medicine production while the old equipment settings are moved to the replacement. This helps the replacement start with the right settings sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Restart delay from configuration migration affects throughput, but this item is capped below the prior batch, keeping both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Restart timing is unpredictable because configuration issues surface only during requalification, so schedules routinely slip. Restart delay from configuration migration is the core error and current practice offers little to shorten the migration-driven downtime. Speeding restart depends on experienced migration teams working in parallel, a scarce capability. Idle sterile fill-finish lines are enormously expensive per day, so delays translate directly into lost production value.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required disposal approvals or notifications omitted from the submission plan
rows.plain: Include every required approval and notice for disposing of the equipment in the submission plan. This helps avoid delays or violations during disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal approvals omitted from the submission plan is a compliance-paperwork gap peripheral to batching with contained consequences; ceiling of 1.0 enforced.
rows.sat_rat: Established EHS checklists cover common approvals but omissions still occur for unusual materials or jurisdictions. Building the full list of required approvals and notifications takes time researching applicable jurisdictions. Identifying every required approval demands regulatory affairs expertise that varies by site. Omitted notifications risk penalties and rework costing time and money.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that approval applications are rejected for incomplete or inaccurate system-disposal information
rows.plain: Make sure disposal applications contain complete and accurate information about the system being discarded. This helps prevent the applications from being rejected.
rows.imp_rat: Disposal-approval rejections for incomplete information are administrative end-of-life paperwork, peripheral to the CFJ of batching liquid product (low relevance); consequences are delay rather than harm and this batch is capped at 1.0 (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Standard application templates reduce errors but incomplete or inaccurate system information still triggers periodic rejections. Correcting rejected applications and resubmitting lengthens the disposal timeline. Accurate system-disposal applications require experienced regulatory preparers. Rejections cause delay-driven storage and re-filing costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of regulatory review beyond the planned decommissioning start date
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting for government approval to dispose of equipment after the planned shutdown date. This helps the shutdown stay on schedule.
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory-review delay past decommissioning start is a schedule/compliance issue at end-of-life, low relevance to core batching; ceiling forces both proxies to 1.
rows.sat_rat: Review durations are highly variable and unpredictable, making schedule slippage the norm rather than the exception. Regulatory review of decommissioning routinely overruns planned start dates because agency timelines are outside the operator's control. Skilled regulatory-affairs staff can shorten review, but such expertise is scarce and unevenly available. Extended review keeps assets and staff in limbo, accruing standby and holding costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of conflict between environmental and safety authorities over applicable disposal requirements
rows.plain: Resolve differences between environmental and safety officials about how the waste must be disposed of. This prevents delays and helps ensure the disposal is accepted by all authorities.
rows.imp_rat: Conflict between authorities over disposal requirements is a regulatory-coordination concern at end-of-life, peripheral to batching; held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Jurisdictional overlap between environmental and safety authorities produces genuine conflicting requirements with no reliable arbitration mechanism, a chronic pain. Resolving conflicts between environmental and safety authorities drags out approvals unpredictably. Reconciling competing regulatory regimes requires specialized legal and compliance judgement that few disposal teams possess. Prolonged inter-agency conflict inflates consulting and delay costs with no clear owner.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that an approval or permit expires before disposal activities begin
rows.plain: Make sure the required approval or permit is still valid when disposal begins. This prevents disposal work from starting without permission.
rows.imp_rat: An expiring disposal permit is a decommissioning scheduling issue distant from the batching CFJ (low relevance); ceiling holds severity at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Calendar and compliance-tracking tools reliably flag expirations for most sites, though coordination lapses occasionally let permits lapse. Permit expiry before disposal begins is fundamentally a timing/scheduling failure between approval and execution. Tracking permit validity windows needs organized regulatory administration. An expired permit forces costly re-application and idle-asset storage.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that decommissioning activities begin before review findings and protective requirements are finalized
rows.plain: Make sure equipment is not taken out of service before the safety review is complete and all needed protections are decided.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning beginning before findings are finalized is an end-of-life sequencing risk, marginal to batching operations; held at 1.0 under the mandatory ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Hold-point and permit-to-work practices exist to gate decommissioning, but schedule pressure and informal starts still cause premature activity in many cases. Waiting for review findings before starting can delay work, creating pressure to begin prematurely. Judging when protective requirements are truly finalized requires regulatory and safety expertise that is not always on hand. Starting before finalization risks abortive work and remediation costs that dwarf the delay avoided.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of system records that are missing or inconsistent with the end-of-life configuration
rows.plain: Avoid missing records or records that do not match the system’s final setup. This helps people identify remaining safety concerns accurately.
rows.imp_rat: Records inconsistent with end-of-life configuration is a decommissioning documentation error, minimally relevant to the batching job; held at 1.0 by the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Configuration records commonly drift from reality over a system's life and are seldom fully reconciled at end-of-life, so inconsistencies are frequent. Reconciling records to the actual end-of-life configuration is time-consuming because as-built drift accumulates over years. Detecting divergence between documented and actual configuration requires experienced eyes familiar with the specific system's modification history. Field verification to correct configuration records adds labor cost that is rarely budgeted at disposal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of identification markings that remain legible after removal or obliteration
rows.plain: Make sure old identification marks are truly unreadable after they are removed, so the equipment cannot be mistaken for something else.
rows.imp_rat: Legibility of markings after obliteration is a decommissioning-marking detail with low relevance to preparing liquids and slight consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Without a defined legibility standard or check, partial or still-readable markings routinely remain, so outcome consistency is only moderate. Obliterating markings by grinding or painting is a quick step within teardown. Obliteration requires little expertise, just adherence to a defacement standard. Consumables and labor for obliteration are inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required regulatory or safety markings removed unintentionally
rows.plain: Prevent legally required or safety labels from being removed by mistake. These markings help people identify the equipment and handle it safely.
rows.imp_rat: Unintended removal of regulatory markings occurs during decommissioning, distant from the batching CFJ, with limited consequence; capped at 1.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Ad hoc marking practices and unclear retain-lists make unintended removal a recurring and poorly controlled error. Distinguishing markings to keep versus remove slows crews who must pause and verify each one. Correctly identifying which regulatory or safety markings must be preserved demands trained judgement that decommissioning crews often lack. Accidental removal of required markings triggers re-labeling and compliance rework costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of damaging system surfaces or components while stripping markings
rows.plain: Remove labels or markings without scratching or otherwise harming the equipment. This helps keep the equipment in good working condition.
rows.imp_rat: Damaging surfaces while stripping markings is a decommissioning-handling error peripheral to preparing liquids, with recoverable consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Outcomes vary with operator care since there is little standardized non-damaging removal procedure. Careful stripping to avoid surface damage slows the work versus aggressive removal. Avoiding damage to sensitive product-contact or cleanroom surfaces while stripping markings requires skilled, attentive technicians. Surface damage during disposal matters less for scrap but can devalue salvageable or refurbishable components.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of removed tags and plates remaining recoverable from the waste stream
rows.plain: Reduce the chance that removed labels and nameplates can still be recovered from the waste. This helps prevent the equipment from being identified after disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Recoverable tags and plates in the waste stream is an end-of-life disposal concern far from the batching CFJ, with minor consequences; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Practice varies widely and recoverable tags frequently slip through because destruction is rarely verified downstream. Destroying or defacing tags adds only minor time to the disposal workflow. Physical destruction of plates is low-skill work needing basic tools and simple instructions. Grinding, shredding, or scrapping tags is cheap, though verifying destruction in the waste stream adds modest expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of adhesive residue or label debris left on cleanroom-facing or product-contact surfaces
rows.plain: Leave as little glue, ink, or label material as possible on surfaces that face the clean work area or touch the product. This helps keep those surfaces clean and safe.
rows.imp_rat: Adhesive residue on product-contact surfaces touches contamination but sits below the batch above in ranking, so relevance and severity are held at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Established cleaning practices handle most residue, but stubborn adhesive on critical surfaces still leaves recurring gaps. Residue cleanup is a defined but time-consuming step in surface reconditioning. Solvent selection and cleanroom-grade cleaning to remove adhesive need moderately trained cleaning staff. Cleaning agents and labor add modest cost, greater if product-contact validation is involved.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of agreements supporting active batching systems terminated by mistake during decommissioning
rows.plain: Avoid accidentally ending agreements that are still needed by systems that are actively combining or preparing batches during equipment removal. This keeps those systems working while they are still needed.
rows.imp_rat: Accidentally terminating agreements for still-active systems during decommissioning could disrupt operations, but it is a lifecycle-admin error below prior items (low relevance); ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Poor asset-to-contract linkage occasionally causes wrong terminations, but consequences are usually caught and corrected, limiting frequency. Verifying which agreements belong to which system before terminating adds modest time. Distinguishing agreements supporting active versus retired systems requires accurate asset-to-contract mapping expertise. Erroneously terminating a live-system agreement can trigger reinstatement fees and service gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of linked service, calibration, maintenance, and consumable supply arrangements left active after system retirement
rows.plain: Make sure all connected service, testing, repair, and supply arrangements are ended when the system is retired. This prevents payments and deliveries from continuing after they are no longer needed.
rows.imp_rat: Leaving service and consumable arrangements active after retirement is a wind-down housekeeping error, not job-critical to batching (low relevance); ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: There is rarely a single register linking arrangements to assets, so dangling active contracts are a frequent oversight. Identifying and cancelling every linked arrangement takes time but is not the primary loss. Tracking down all service, calibration, and consumable contracts tied to a system needs procurement knowledge that is often incomplete. Arrangements left active after retirement bleed recurring fees for services no longer needed, a direct and common waste.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of required decommissioning support unavailability after premature agreement termination
rows.plain: Keep the help needed to shut down the equipment available for longer if connected services end earlier than planned. This helps prevent delays during the shutdown.
rows.imp_rat: Decommissioning-support unavailability after premature termination is an end-of-life timing error not central to batching (low relevance); batch ranking caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: When premature termination occurs the resulting support gap is somewhat predictable and usually recoverable, so the exposure is bounded. Restoring support after a mistaken termination introduces lead-time gaps until service is re-established. Re-establishing terminated support requires vendor-relationship handling that most teams can manage. Support unavailability may force costly interim workarounds, but this is secondary to the downtime itself.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of avoidable termination fees incurred when contractual notice deadlines are missed
rows.plain: Avoid unnecessary fees for ending services when required notice dates are missed. This reduces the cost of stopping services that are no longer needed.
