rows.plain: Make sure the notice arrives early enough for everyone affected to prepare for the system’s removal.
rows.imp_rat: Late notice for system removal is a communication-timing concern (relevance 1) with modest consequences (severity 1); capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Planned shutdowns follow project timelines with scheduled notifications, though ad hoc removals still occasionally surprise stakeholders. The core error is late notice, and standard project scheduling with advance decommissioning plans usually gives stakeholders reasonable lead time. Issuing timely notices is a routine coordination task requiring little special skill. Late notice can force rushed workarounds, but the notification itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of critical removal details missing from the notice
rows.plain: Avoid leaving important information about removing the equipment out of the notice. Complete instructions help everyone affected understand what will be removed and what they need to do.
rows.imp_rat: Missing critical removal details in a notice can cause rework during decommissioning, but this administrative error ranks below the prior batch and is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Standardized MOC notice templates capture most critical details, but freeform notices routinely omit specifics. Assembling complete removal details is quick when a template drives the notice. Populating critical details needs someone who understands the removal scope, a modest expertise requirement. Missing details cause avoidable rework and coordination cost during removal.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency in removal dates, scope, or responsibilities communicated across operations, quality, and safety teams
rows.plain: Make sure the operations, quality, and safety teams receive the same information about when equipment will be removed, what the work includes, and who is responsible.
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistency in removal dates/scope across teams is a decommissioning coordination error only loosely tied to the CFJ; consequences are communication friction. Held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Communication commonly relies on email and siloed schedules, so inconsistencies across teams are frequent and hard to catch. Reconciling removal dates and scope across teams consumes repeated meetings and delays alignment. Coordinating operations, quality, and safety requires practiced cross-functional facilitation. Inconsistent information causes rework and standby costs when teams act on conflicting plans.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that stakeholder receipt and understanding remain unconfirmed before removal begins
rows.plain: Make sure everyone affected has received and understood the notice before removal starts. This helps them prepare and avoid surprises.
rows.imp_rat: Unconfirmed stakeholder receipt before removal is a coordination unreliability; consequences are recoverable and it sits below the prior batch, capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Most notices assume receipt via email without documented acknowledgment, so genuine confirmation of understanding is frequently unverified. Chasing confirmations of receipt and understanding adds coordination time before removal can proceed. Confirming understanding is administrative rather than skill-intensive. Unconfirmed understanding is cheap to close but expensive if it leads to unsafe or disruptive removal.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the batching system is routed to the wrong destination because return-channel instructions conflict
rows.plain: The batching system should be sent to the correct destination, even if different return instructions are provided. This prevents the equipment from being returned to the wrong place.
rows.imp_rat: Misrouting the system due to conflicting return-channel instructions is a reverse-logistics error remote from the core preparation job (low relevance); the batch ceiling holds both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Return channels for retired systems are often documented inconsistently across contracts and vendors, so wrong-destination routing happens with partial safeguards only. Sorting out conflicting return-channel instructions delays dispatch while destinations are confirmed. Interpreting which return instruction governs requires logistics and contract familiarity that varies by team. Misrouting incurs re-shipping, storage, and penalty costs that are wholly avoidable.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the return is refused because eligibility or authorization records are incomplete
rows.plain: The equipment should only be returned when the records show that it is allowed and properly authorized for return. This prevents the return from being refused.
rows.imp_rat: Return refusal from incomplete authorization records is an end-of-life administrative error not central to batching (low relevance); the ceiling caps both proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Eligibility and authorization records are frequently incomplete because they are assembled reactively, so refusals recur despite established return processes. A refused return forces re-submission cycles that stall the disposal timeline. Assembling complete eligibility and authorization records demands knowledge of the specific return program's rules. Refusals cause repeated logistics and storage costs while paperwork is corrected.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of damage to the batching system during disconnection, removal, or transit
rows.plain: Prevent the batching system from being damaged while it is disconnected, removed, or transported. This helps keep the equipment usable when it is returned.
rows.imp_rat: Damage during removal or transit affects asset integrity, yet the ranking places this below the prior batch, capping proxies at 1.
rows.sat_rat: Established rigging and crating practices keep gross damage uncommon, though minor damage during disconnection is routine. Protecting the system during removal and transit adds handling time but is normal rigging work. Experienced riggers and movers handle skid-mounted systems competently, though attention to fragile instrumentation varies. Since the batching system is being disposed of, transit damage is less costly unless resale or recycling value is impaired.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of return delay caused by misaligned site access, pickup, and receiving availability
rows.plain: Spend less time waiting to return equipment because the site, pickup service, and receiving location are not ready at the same time. Better coordination helps the equipment get back sooner.
rows.imp_rat: Return delay from misaligned access/pickup/receiving is a reverse-logistics scheduling error peripheral to batching function; consequences are timeline delays. Ceiling applies.
rows.sat_rat: Standard logistics scheduling handles most returns, though occasional misalignment still causes avoidable delays. Return delays from misaligned site access, pickup, and receiving windows are common but usually resolved within days. Coordinating logistics is routine scheduling work needing little specialized skill. Delays incur modest demurrage or storage costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required decommissioning records left incomplete or inconsistent at project closure
rows.plain: Complete and keep consistent all records needed when the system is taken out of use at the end of the project. This prevents confusion about what was done and leaves a reliable closing record.
rows.imp_rat: Incomplete decommissioning records at closure is a paperwork-consistency error largely peripheral to liquid batching with modest impact; capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checklists and closeout procedures exist but consistency is poor because verification is manual and frequently rushed at project end. Compiling complete decommissioning records at closure is slow and often deferred until teams are already dispersed. Knowing exactly which records are required and cross-checking them demands experienced project-quality personnel who are often unavailable at closeout. Incomplete records force costly reconstruction, re-inspection, or regulatory follow-up later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of decontamination records missing evidence required for disposal approval
rows.plain: Make sure the cleaning records include all proof needed to approve disposal. Without this evidence, the material may not be cleared for disposal.
rows.imp_rat: Decontamination records missing disposal-approval evidence is a recordkeeping gap peripheral to the batching job with contained consequences; held to 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Decontamination records are often incomplete for end-of-life equipment because cleaning was not documented for disposal purposes, so gaps recur. Compiling decontamination evidence for disposal approval is a slow records-gathering effort. Knowing which evidence satisfies disposal reviewers requires quality and regulatory experience. Missing records force re-decontamination or re-testing, adding cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of historical batch data and recipe records omitted from the export
rows.plain: Make sure exported records include all past production-group information and written machine instructions, so the history is not missing important details.
rows.imp_rat: Omitted historical batch/recipe data in export undermines record completeness, but it sits below the prior batch and is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Export scoping often misses archived or ancillary datasets, so partial omissions recur despite decent tooling. Bulk exporting historical data is reasonably fast when scoped properly. Ensuring completeness of the export requires knowledge of where all records reside across systems. Omitted records may require costly retrieval from decommissioned systems later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch records are corrupted during extraction or transfer
rows.plain: Make sure production records are not damaged or changed while they are being copied out or moved. The records must remain usable afterward.
rows.imp_rat: Corrupted batch records during extraction threaten data integrity, but this item ranks below the prior batch and is capped at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Standard database export and checksum verification make outright corruption uncommon, though legacy formats occasionally cause issues. Batch record extraction is generally quick with vendor export tools. Running exports needs IT/automation support familiar with the historian or MES, a moderate skill. Corrupted records force costly recovery efforts, but corruption is relatively infrequent.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exported batch histories missing links to their recipe versions and audit trails
rows.plain: Make sure exported production records stay linked to the correct recipe version and history of changes. These links help people understand how each batch was made and what was changed.
rows.imp_rat: Exported histories missing recipe/audit links weaken traceability, yet ranking below prior batch forces a cap at 1/1.
rows.sat_rat: Exports commonly flatten data and sever links between batches, recipe versions, and audit trails, leaving this poorly served. Re-linking batch histories to recipe versions and audit trails manually is time-consuming when links break on export. Preserving relational links requires data-migration expertise in the specific system architecture. Broken links drive expensive reconstruction and compliance risk.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that exported records cannot be opened or interpreted after the control platform is retired
rows.plain: Make sure the transferred production records can still be opened and understood after the machine’s control software is retired.
rows.imp_rat: Inability to open exported records after platform retirement is an archival/end-of-life concern with low relevance to running batching operations; severity capped at 1 under the batch ordering.
rows.sat_rat: Proprietary, undocumented export formats routinely become unreadable once the platform is retired, and this is a chronic, largely unmitigated failure in the disposal stage. Data migration from a retired control platform is rarely planned ahead, so teams scramble at end-of-life with no time budget for format conversion. Interpreting proprietary export formats after the platform is gone demands scarce specialist knowledge that usually leaves with the vendor or the departed engineer. Recovering or re-hosting unreadable records later is far more expensive than exporting to open formats upfront, but that spend is rarely provisioned.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of misalignment between export verification and decommissioning schedules
rows.plain: Make sure the timing for checking exported batches matches the timing for taking the related equipment out of service, so records are checked before they are no longer available.
rows.imp_rat: Misalignment between export verification and decommissioning schedules is an end-of-life coordination error peripheral to batching; consequences are compliance delay. Capped at 1.0.
rows.sat_rat: Export control processes exist but are often coordinated separately from decommissioning, so schedule misalignment recurs. Aligning export verification with the decommissioning schedule can introduce timing gaps that delay shipment. Export compliance verification requires specialized trade-compliance expertise that is not always embedded in decommissioning teams. Misalignment risks holds, penalties, or storage costs on export-bound equipment.
